name: ShiftPay
description: ShiftPay is a cloud‑native payroll platform built for multi‑unit restaurant groups. It syncs with POS, time‑clock and scheduling tools to calculate wages, tip credits and taxes in real‑time, then files and pays employees with a single click.
target_customer: Restaurant groups (10‑200 locations) that pay for ShiftPay to eliminate manual payroll processing, reduce compliance risk, and free managers to focus on service, not spreadsheets.
pricing: Subscription‑based – $45 per location per month + $2 per active employee for tip‑credit calculations. Minimum contract is one month, with a 10% discount for annual pre‑pay.
traction: Pilot launched 6 months ago with three regional restaurant groups (totaling 85 locations and 4,200 employees). We've processed $2.1 M in payroll, achieved 92% on‑time payment accuracy, and earned an NPS of 71. Two of the pilots have already signed three‑year contracts, and we’re in talks with two national chains for beta‑testing next quarter.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel spent 7 years as a senior analyst at a Big‑4 firm, leading payroll automation projects for hospitality clients; CTO Luis Gomez previously built real‑time data pipelines at a leading restaurant POS vendor.
problem: Restaurant groups still compile hours from multiple sources (POS, handheld clocks, tip pools), then run a manual, error‑prone payroll process that costs $1,200 per location per month in labor and exposes them to costly compliance penalties.
solution: ShiftPay pulls raw shift data directly from POS and scheduling software, auto‑calculates tip credits, overtime and multi‑state tax rules, and pushes the final payroll to banks and tax agencies. Managers get a dashboard that flags exceptions instantly, so the entire payroll cycle can be completed in under two hours for any number of locations.

name: CrossPay
description: CrossPay is a web‑based platform that tries to make cross‑border payments easier for freelancers working across Southeast Asian markets. We connect to a handful of local banks and e‑wallets and claim to reduce friction.
target_customer: Freelancers and small agencies in countries like Indonesia, Vietnam and the Philippines who need to receive payments from overseas clients and are looking for a simpler way to move money.
pricing: We plan to charge a small markup on each transaction (around 0.5‑1% of the amount) plus a flat $1‑$2 service fee per payout, though exact numbers are still being finalized.
traction: We have a prototype and a few informal talks with potential users, but no signed contracts or revenue to date.
team: Founded by a former accountant who worked at a regional bank and a software engineer with experience in payment APIs.
problem: Freelancers in Southeast Asia often face high fees, slow settlement times, and lack of a unified way to receive money from foreign clients.
solution: CrossPay aggregates multiple payment rails into a single dashboard, allowing users to request, receive, and convert foreign payments with a single click.

name: BuilderSpend
description: BuilderSpend is a cloud‑based spend‑management platform that helps construction subcontractors track, approve and reimburse purchases in real time. We integrate directly with the tools they already use (QuickBooks, Sage, and popular card providers) to turn chaotic receipt piles into clean, auditable expense data.
target_customer: Subcontractors on small‑to‑mid‑size projects (typically $1‑10 M contracts) pay for the service because it reduces admin time, speeds up reimbursements, and protects margins against off‑budget overruns.
pricing: We charge a flat $49 per active user per month plus a 0.3% transaction fee on card‑linked spend; enterprise tiers start at $499/mo for unlimited users and custom integrations.
traction: We’ve run a 3‑month pilot with two subcontractors (one electrical, one plumbing) who have logged $120k in spend through the platform and report a 30% cut in processing time. We’re currently in talks with three additional firms and have a waiting list of two more.
team: Founder Sam Patel was a project manager on large commercial builds before moving into product management at a SaaS payments startup; CTO Lina Gomez has 7 years of full‑stack experience building fintech APIs. We’re a two‑person team now, planning to hire a UX designer next quarter.
problem: Subcontractors still rely on spreadsheets, paper receipts and manual approvals, causing delayed reimbursements, lost documentation and cost overruns that bleed profit margins.
solution: BuilderSpend digitizes every purchase via mobile receipt capture, auto‑matches spend to job codes, routes approvals through an intuitive dashboard and syncs the final data to the subcontractor’s existing accounting system, cutting admin overhead and making spend transparent to owners.

name: SmilePay
description: SmilePay is a buy‑now‑pay‑later platform built specifically for dental practices. We embed a seamless credit line into the clinic’s scheduling and billing flow, letting patients spread the cost of procedures over 3‑12 months while the practice gets paid up‑front.
target_customer: Dental clinics are the direct buyers – they pay a fee to access the financing line and keep their cash flow intact. The end‑users are patients who want to avoid large out‑of‑pocket payments at the point of care.
pricing: We’re still finalising the exact model. The pilot has used a hybrid of a small per‑transaction markup (≈1.5‑2% of the financed amount) plus an optional monthly subscription (around $200‑$400 per practice). The exact mix will be locked in after we complete the next round of pricing experiments.
traction: In the past six months we ran pilots with 7 mid‑size dental groups, processing $420k in financed procedures and seeing a 22% increase in average appointment value. Two clinics have already signed LOIs for a full‑rollout, and we’ve secured a $750k seed round to scale.
team: I’m Alex Rivera, former product lead at a dental SaaS startup and a former analyst at Goldman Sachs where I built credit risk models. Co‑founder Maya Patel ran the payments division at a national health‑tech platform and has deep relationships with dental associations.
problem: Patients often postpone or cancel high‑cost dental work because they can’t afford the lump‑sum payment, and clinics lose revenue and have to manage complex third‑party financing.
solution: SmilePay integrates directly with practice management software, runs an instant credit decision engine, and offers patients a transparent, interest‑free repayment schedule. The clinic receives the full bill immediately, while patients pay over time, boosting case acceptance and practice cash flow.

name: SpendSync
description: SpendSync is a lightweight SaaS that automatically pulls credit‑card feeds, receipt images, and project codes from the tools remote‑first dev teams already use, then reconciles expenses to the right budget line in seconds. It’s built to sit in Slack and pull data from the same APIs that power their daily stand‑ups, so finance never has to ask “who bought this?” again.
target_customer: The paying user is the CFO or Finance Ops lead of a remote‑first software studio (typically 30‑80 employees) that already spends $50‑$200k per year on third‑party expense tools and wants to cut manual reconciliation time.
pricing: Subscription per active user, $15 / month for up to 5 users, $30 / month for 6‑15 users, with a $300 annual enterprise add‑on for custom integrations and audit logs.
traction: In the last 3 months we have 12 paying studios, averaging a 4‑hour per week reduction in manual expense work and a $2,000 annual cost saving per team. The churn is 0% to date, and we’ve built a waiting list of 8 more studios that are on a rolling beta.
team: I’m Alex Patel, former senior analyst at Stripe who built the expense API that powers their internal finance automation, and co‑founder Maya Liu, a full‑stack engineer who previously shipped the receipt‑OCR pipeline for a B2B travel startup.
problem: Remote‑first teams generate a constant stream of tiny, cross‑border expenses that land in personal cards or shared virtual cards, forcing finance to chase receipts across Slack, email, and individual bank portals – a process that eats up weeks of labor for a handful of engineers.
solution: SpendSync hooks into the same card‑issuance APIs, uses OCR to extract receipt data, matches each line‑item to a project tag from the team’s issue tracker, and posts a reconciliation summary back to the originating Slack channel. Finance can approve or reject with one click, and the data is instantly pushed to the company’s ERP.

name: FreightCash
description: FreightCash provides on‑demand invoice factoring for small‑to‑mid‑size trucking fleets, turning 30‑day freight invoices into cash in under 24 hours. We integrate directly with carriers’ TMS and accounting software, so drivers and fleet managers can keep trucks moving without waiting for customer payments.
target_customer: Fleet owners and operators (typically 5‑50 trucks) who pay factoring fees to free up working capital and avoid missed loads caused by cash‑flow gaps.
pricing: We charge a flat 3% factor fee on the invoice amount plus a $15 transaction fee per invoice; volume discounts kick in after $500k of invoices processed per month.
traction: In the past 6 months we’ve onboarded 38 fleets (average 12 trucks each) processing $2.1M of invoices, with a 94% repeat rate and $120k in ARR. Our pilot with a regional carrier reduced payment cycle time from 45 to 2 days, and we have LOIs for $1.5M of additional invoice volume in Q3.
team: I’m Alex Rivera, former product lead at a $300M logistics SaaS startup and ex‑investment analyst at a freight‑focused venture fund; co‑founder Maya Patel brings 8 years of senior finance at a national carrier and built the core underwriting engine.
problem: Trucking fleets often wait 30‑60 days for shippers to pay invoices, forcing them to dip into high‑interest lines or idle trucks, which erodes margins and limits growth.
solution: FreightCash purchases approved invoices instantly, advances 95% of the amount via a secure API, and handles collections, giving fleets immediate cash to cover fuel, maintenance and driver pay while we earn a transparent, usage‑based fee.

name: CryptoTaxEasy
description: CryptoTaxEasy is a web‑app that helps retail crypto traders generate tax reports with a few clicks. We aim to simplify the messy process of figuring out gains, losses and the corresponding tax liability across multiple exchanges.
target_customer: Retail crypto traders who need to file taxes and are willing to pay for a convenient, automated report instead of doing the math manually or hiring an accountant.
pricing: Subscription model – $19/month for basic filing assistance, $49/month for advanced multi‑exchange support and audit‑ready PDFs.
traction: We have a handful of sign‑ups from friends and a few users from an early‑stage Discord community, but no paid customers or measurable growth yet.
team: Founded by me, Alex Rivera, who spent a couple of years trading crypto and built a small data‑scraping tool for personal use.
problem: Retail crypto traders struggle to calculate taxable events across dozens of wallets and exchanges, leading to errors and wasted time during tax season.
solution: Our platform connects to exchange APIs, pulls transaction data, categorises trades and automatically generates a formatted tax report that can be uploaded to standard filing software.

name: CrossCover
description: CrossCover is a lightweight SaaS platform that lets small‑to‑medium importers lock in foreign‑exchange rates in minutes, without needing a treasury desk. We combine a simple UI with API hooks to the best liquidity providers, giving users real‑time quotes, automated “stop‑loss” hedges and post‑trade reporting.
target_customer: The paying customers are the finance or operations heads of import‑focused SMEs (annual revenue $5‑50 M) that regularly buy goods in EUR, CNY or MXN and need to protect thin margins from FX volatility.
pricing: We charge a flat subscription of $149/mo per active currency pair, plus a 0.15 % per‑transaction fee on hedged trades (capped at $500 per month). A 30‑day free trial is available.
traction: In the last 3 months we have signed 12 pilot contracts (average $2k ARR each) and processed $1.2 M of hedged volume, with a churn‑free rate of 92 % among those pilots. Our referral pipeline is modest but steady; we’re still waiting on a larger distributor partnership to scale.
team: I (Laura Chen) am a former FX dealer at a Tier‑1 bank and built the core risk‑engine during my time at a treasury software startup. Our CTO, Arjun Patel, left a payments unicorn where he led API security; we’re currently a two‑person founding team plus a part‑time UX consultant.
problem: Small importers cannot afford the overhead of traditional FX hedging desks and are forced to either absorb volatile currency swings or rely on expensive, opaque broker spreads.
solution: CrossCover gives them an affordable, self‑service hedge: users get instant market rates, can set automated forward contracts or stop‑loss triggers, and the platform executes the trade through our partnered liquidity pool, all from a dashboard they can embed in their ERP.

name: GigBank
description: GigBank embeds a full‑stack banking layer directly into gig‑platform APIs, giving freelancers instant accounts, instant payouts, and built‑in compliance tools. We let platforms turn any worker into a banking customer without building a bank from scratch.
target_customer: The gig platforms themselves pay us because they need a turnkey banking solution that reduces churn and compliance costs, while the freelancers get free accounts and faster cash‑flow.
pricing: We’re currently experimenting with a mixed model: a small per‑active‑user fee (around $0.10‑$0.25 per month) plus a modest per‑transaction markup (0.5‑1 % of payout). The exact tiers are still being validated with our pilot partners, so we don’t have firm price points yet.
traction: Beta launched with two mid‑size ride‑share apps, processing $1.2 M in payouts in the first 8 weeks and cutting their payout latency from 48 h to under 5 min. Both partners have signed letters of intent for expanded rollout.
team: I’m Maya Patel, former product lead at Stripe’s Connect team, and co‑founder Luis Gomez, ex‑engineer at Plaid who built the API layer for their ACH product.
problem: Gig platforms struggle to offer native banking services; they either outsource to legacy banks with slow payouts or build costly, compliance‑heavy infrastructure themselves.
solution: GigBank provides a plug‑and‑play API that creates instant digital accounts, handles KYC, ACH, debit card issuance, and real‑time payouts, letting platforms launch banking features in weeks instead of months.

name: CoverClear
description: CoverClear is a SaaS platform that automates insurance‑policy compliance for commercial landlords, flagging gaps and generating state‑specific reporting packages. We pull data from property management systems, match it against local liability requirements, and deliver a single compliance dashboard.
target_customer: The paying customers are small‑to‑mid‑size commercial property owners (typically 5‑50 units) and the property‑management firms that service them. They need it to avoid costly fines and to satisfy lenders who demand proof of coverage.
pricing: We charge a monthly subscription per property – $49 for the first 5 units, then $8 per additional unit. Enterprise‑level accounts (over 200 units) get a negotiated flat fee. A 30‑day free trial is offered.
traction: We’ve closed pilot agreements with three property‑management firms covering 42 landlord portfolios (≈350 units) and have received a letter of intent from a regional bank that will require our compliance reports for its commercial loan pipeline. Our churn on the pilot has been zero so far.
team: I’m the founder, a former insurance analyst at Aon who built a compliance engine for large corporate clients, and my co‑founder is a software engineer with a background in regulatory tech at a fintech incubator.
problem: Commercial landlords must keep up with a patchwork of state and municipal insurance mandates; missing a single requirement can trigger penalties, higher premiums, or loan denial, yet most landlords rely on manual spreadsheets that are error‑prone and not auditable.
solution: CoverClear integrates with existing property‑management software, extracts lease and risk data, runs it through a rules engine that codifies every jurisdiction’s insurance statutes, and produces a compliance score plus the exact policy language landlords need to update. The platform also sends automated alerts when policies lapse or regulations change.

name: FlowFund
description: FlowFund gives nonprofits a real‑time treasury dashboard that links banking, cash‑flow forecasting, and automated disbursements. In minutes they can move idle cash into short‑term yields, schedule recurring grants, and see the impact of every dollar instantly.
target_customer: Chief Financial Officers or Finance Directors at mid‑size NGOs (annual budgets $5‑30 M) who need to stretch donor dollars and report cash‑flow health to boards and funders.
pricing: Subscription‑based SaaS: $250/month for organizations under $10 M in budget, $500/month for $10‑30 M, plus a 0.15 % fee on any automated investment transactions (capped at $250/mo).
traction: Beta launched in Jan 2024 with 5 nonprofit partners handling $2.3 M in transactions; average idle cash reduced by 22 % and reported 15 % ROI on short‑term investments. One pilot secured a $150 k grant from the XYZ Foundation to expand to 20 NGOs before year‑end.
team: I’m Maya Patel, former treasury analyst at a $4B charitable bank and ex‑product lead at a B2B cash‑management startup. Co‑founder Luis Gomez spent 6 years building APIs for regional credit unions and leads engineering.
problem: Nonprofits still manage cash the way legacy charities did a decade ago—manual spreadsheets, delayed bank feeds, and most of their unrestricted cash just sits idle, eroding purchasing power and limiting program impact.
solution: FlowFund integrates directly with banks via APIs to provide live balances, auto‑allocates surplus cash into FDIC‑insured short‑term instruments, and automates scheduled grant payments. The UI ties every transaction to program codes, giving finance teams the data they need for transparent board reports and for donors who want real‑time impact metrics.

name: TruckFin
description: TruckFin is a data‑driven underwriting platform aimed at food‑truck owners who need quick access to working capital. We pull together whatever transaction and location data we can find and feed it into a simple credit model, then connect the trucks with a network of lenders.
target_customer: The paying customers are the lenders – banks, credit unions and alternative finance firms that want to tap into the niche food‑truck market, and they pay us for the underwriting service.
pricing: We take a usage‑based fee: roughly 2–4 % of each loan amount funded through our platform, plus a small monthly subscription for data access (around $100‑$200 per month).
traction: We’ve done a few informal outreach calls and have a pilot with one food‑truck operator that is testing our API; no loans have actually closed yet and we’re still refining the data pipeline.
team: Founder Jane Doe was a credit analyst at a regional bank and saw the gap for mobile vendors; co‑founder John Smith is a full‑stack developer who previously built point‑of‑sale software for small retailers.
problem: Food‑truck owners struggle to get loans because traditional lenders don’t have enough granular data on their cash flow and seasonal demand, leading to high rejection rates or costly collateral requirements.
solution: TruckFin aggregates point‑of‑sale, GPS and vendor‑submitted sales data to create a basic credit score for each truck, then offers that score to partner lenders so they can make faster, lower‑cost decisions.

name: BridgeBank
description: BridgeBank is a mobile‑first financial‑literacy platform built for Gen Z immigrants navigating a new banking system. We combine bite‑sized lessons, bilingual tutorials, and community challenges to help users build credit, budget, and send money home with confidence.
target_customer: The primary payers are the users themselves—young adults aged 18‑25 who are students or first‑time workers—who value a low‑cost, culturally relevant tool to avoid costly banking mistakes. We also sell bulk licenses to colleges and non‑profits that run integration programs.
pricing: Freemium model: free access to core lessons, plus a $4.99/month premium tier unlocking advanced budgeting tools, live Q&A with financial coaches, and ad‑free experience. Bulk institutional pricing starts at $3/user/month for 100+ seats.
traction: Beta launched in March with 5,200 sign‑ups from three metro areas; 12% have upgraded to premium after four weeks. We’ve partnered with two community colleges and a local immigrant advocacy group, and our churn is under 8% for the free tier.
team: I’m a first‑generation immigrant who grew up juggling multiple currencies; after a stint as a product analyst at a large fintech and a year building the prototype solo, I brought on a full‑stack engineer friend. Beyond the two of us we have a part‑time UX designer and an advisory board of three community leaders.
problem: Gen Z immigrants often lack accessible, culturally relevant financial education, leading to under‑utilization of banking services, high fees, and poor credit histories—all of which impede long‑term economic stability.
solution: BridgeBank delivers short, gamified lessons in both English and the user’s native language, integrates directly with major U.S. banks for real‑time budgeting feedback, and creates a peer‑support network where users can share tips and celebrate milestones.

name: Rewardify
description: Rewardify lets SaaS companies embed a programmable corporate card that automatically backs purchases with cash‑back or credit rewards for their end‑users. The card is API‑driven, so you can tie rewards to any usage metric you care about without building a whole payments stack.
target_customer: Growth and finance teams at mid‑market SaaS firms (ARR $5‑50M) who pay for the service to boost customer acquisition, reduce churn, and differentiate their pricing plans.
pricing: We’re still fine‑tuning the model, but today we charge a small per‑card activation fee (≈$5‑$10) plus a usage‑based take‑rate that ranges roughly between 0.5%‑1.5% of transaction volume. We also offer a bundled “enterprise” tier with a flat monthly fee (around $2‑3k) for high‑volume customers, but the exact numbers will evolve as we learn from pilots.
traction: In the last 4 months we’ve signed 5 beta SaaS partners, processed $210k in spend, and saw an average 12% lift in user retention for those pilots. Two of the partners have already committed to a paid roll‑out once pricing is solidified.
team: I’m Alex Chen, former senior product manager on Stripe’s corporate card team; co‑founder Maya Patel was VP of growth at a $200M SaaS firm and built its first loyalty program.
problem: SaaS companies want to reward loyal users but face high integration costs, compliance headaches, and lack of flexibility with existing card or rewards providers.
solution: Rewardify provides a single, compliant virtual card that can be provisioned instantly via API and automatically issues rewards based on any custom event (e.g., usage minutes, subscription upgrades), eliminating the need for separate payment processors and loyalty platforms.

name: ShiftSave
description: ShiftSave is a mobile‑first micro‑investment app built for hourly workers who get paid per shift. It automatically rounds up each paycheck to the nearest dollar and invests the spare change in diversified ETFs, letting users grow a nest‑egg without any upfront lump sum.
target_customer: The paying customer is the hourly employee (e.g., restaurant staff, retail associates, gig drivers) who wants to start saving but finds traditional investing intimidating and unaffordable.
pricing: We charge a flat 0.5 % management fee on assets under management, with a $1 minimum monthly contribution; there are no account‑opening fees and no withdrawal penalties.
traction: In our 3‑month beta we onboarded 215 users (average $24/month contributions) and grew AUM to $5.8k. 78 % of participants have stayed active after the first month, and referrals from a single restaurant chain have added a steady trickle of new users.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel was a senior software engineer at Stripe and previously managed a high‑volume coffee shop; she combines fintech product chops with first‑hand knowledge of shift‑work cash flow challenges.
problem: Hourly workers earn irregular, small paychecks and lack the tools or confidence to set aside money for long‑term investing, often ending up with “pay‑day” spikes and no systematic savings.
solution: ShiftSave links directly to payroll providers and automatically rounds up each shift’s net pay, depositing the excess into a low‑cost ETF portfolio that can be withdrawn any time, giving workers a painless, always‑on savings habit.

name: TheraPay
description: TheraPay builds simple, HIPAA‑compliant payment links that let independent therapists get paid in seconds, right after a session. The links embed in booking confirmations, email reminders, and even telehealth platforms, removing the need for paper invoices or cash handling.
target_customer: Independent counselors, psychologists, physical therapists and other solo‑practitioners who bill clients directly and need a quick, secure way to collect fees without a full‑blown practice management system.
pricing: We charge a 1.5% transaction fee plus a flat $0.10 per payment, with an optional $29/month premium plan that adds custom branding, automated receipt emails, and API access. The base model is free‑to‑start with the per‑transaction fee only.
traction: Beta launched 3 months ago with 215 therapists across four states. We’re processing $12,300 MRR, have a 92% monthly retention rate, and the average therapist reports a 30% reduction in admin time. Three therapists have already upgraded to the premium plan after the first month.
team: I’m Alex Chen, former senior engineer at Stripe and product lead on their Payments API, and co‑founder Maya Patel, a licensed clinical social worker who ran her own solo practice for 8 years.
problem: Solo therapists still rely on manual invoicing, cash, or generic payment apps that lack HIPAA compliance and don’t integrate with their scheduling workflow, causing delayed payments and extra admin work.
solution: TheraPay generates a one‑click payment link that can be attached to any booking confirmation or telehealth session. The link routes through a PCI‑DSS and HIPAA‑compliant gateway, sends an automatic receipt, and reconciles directly into the therapist’s bank account, cutting payment friction to under 5 seconds.

name: SecureCart AI
description: SecureCart AI builds a machine‑learning platform that watches transactions on peer‑to‑peer marketplaces and flags suspicious activity. We aim to give sellers and marketplace operators a simple dashboard to keep fraud under control without huge engineering effort.
target_customer: Marketplace operators and large sellers who pay a monthly fee to protect their revenue and reputation.
pricing: Subscription‑based: $499/mo for basic monitoring, $1,299/mo for advanced analytics and API access, with custom enterprise quotes.
traction: We ran a short pilot with a single marketplace in Q1 2024, but we haven’t yet signed any paying customers or secured a pipeline.
team: Founder Alex Rivera, former data analyst at a payment processor, previously built small fraud‑detection scripts for e‑commerce sites.
problem: Online marketplaces lose millions each year to fraudulent listings, chargebacks and identity theft, and current tools are either too generic or require heavy integration.
solution: Our SaaS platform ingests transaction data, applies a proprietary risk model, and returns real‑time fraud scores that can be acted on via a web UI or API.

name: ScoreLite
description: ScoreLite builds AI‑driven credit scores for borrowers who have little or no traditional credit history. We combine alternative data—utility payments, rental receipts, and transactional patterns—with a lightweight risk model that lenders can embed via a simple API.
target_customer: Our direct customers are small‑to‑mid sized online lenders and fintech platforms that need to underwrite loan applicants quickly; they pay for the improved approval rates and lower default risk that our scores provide.
pricing: We charge a per‑score fee of $0.15‑$0.30 depending on volume, with a minimum monthly commitment of $5,000 for up to 20,000 scores. Enterprise contracts can negotiate a flat‑rate subscription after 100k scores per month.
traction: In the past six months we have integrated with two micro‑loan apps and a rent‑payment platform, processing ~12k scores and seeing a 22% lift in approved volume for those partners. Our churn is currently zero but the pipeline is shallow and the market is saturated with similar alternatives.
team: I’m the founder, a former data scientist at a major credit bureau who built fraud models for two years; my co‑founder is a full‑stack engineer who left a neobank after a 12‑month stint. We have no dedicated sales or compliance hires yet.
problem: Thin‑file borrowers—students, gig workers, recent immigrants—are systematically excluded from credit because traditional bureaus lack enough data, forcing lenders to either reject them or take on excessive risk.
solution: ScoreLite aggregates consented alternative data sources, normalises them, and runs a proprietary machine‑learning model that outputs a credit score compatible with existing underwriting workflows, letting lenders confidently extend credit to these underserved segments.

name: RevBoost
description: RevBoost is a Shopify‑native financing platform that gives D2C merchants quick access to growth capital in exchange for a share of their future sales. We embed the funding request directly in the store admin, so founders can pull a loan in minutes and see the repayment schedule update in real‑time.
target_customer: Our paying customers are early‑stage Shopify store owners (usually 6‑30 M ARR) who need cash to scale inventory, marketing or hiring but can’t or don’t want to take on traditional debt.
pricing: We take a revenue‑share on the funded amount, roughly between 8 %–12 % of monthly gross sales until the loan is repaid. The exact % is still being calibrated based on store risk profile, so we’re currently piloting a tiered model and will lock in rates once we have enough data.
traction: Since our beta launch 3 months ago we have funded $1.2 M across 45 stores, with an average repayment horizon of 10 months and a Net Promoter Score of 68. Two of our early merchants have reported 30 % YoY revenue growth after using our capital, and we have a wait‑list of 120+ qualified shops.
team: I’m Alex Chen, former head of product at Klarna and a Shopify merchant myself. Co‑founder Maya Patel spent five years building risk models for Square and leads our underwriting engine.
problem: Fast‑growing D2C brands often run out of cash between inventory orders and the delay of traditional loans, forcing them to rely on high‑cost credit cards or give up equity.
solution: RevBoost offers a flexible, revenue‑based loan that auto‑repays a fixed % of daily sales, eliminating the need for fixed monthly payments and preserving founder equity while providing the liquidity needed to seize growth opportunities.

name: LedgerLaw
description: LedgerLaw is a SaaS platform that automates accounts payable and receivable for boutique law firms. By plugging directly into practice‑management tools, we turn scanned invoices and settlement notices into booked transactions without a clerk touching a spreadsheet.
target_customer: Boutique law firms (10‑50 attorneys) that bill hourly or on contingency and currently rely on a handful of admins to process invoices. They pay because every hour saved on AP/AR is billable time back for lawyers and reduces compliance risk.
pricing: Flat‑fee per firm: $500 /month for up to 20 users, plus $0.08 per processed invoice after the first 500. Optional add‑on for custom integration at $1,200 /quarter.
traction: In a 6‑month closed beta we onboarded 3 firms handling $210 k of monthly invoice volume. Clients report a 30‑40% drop in processing time and 1‑2 % decrease in billing errors. We have a wait‑list of two additional firms for Q3 rollout.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel spent 7 years running legal operations at a mid‑size firm and built the AP workflow that inspired LedgerLaw; CTO Alex Chen is a former senior engineer at Stripe who led their invoicing APIs.
problem: Law firms still process invoices, retainers and settlement payouts on paper or in disparate spreadsheets, leading to duplicate entry, missed deadlines and costly compliance headaches.
solution: LedgerLaw uses OCR and machine‑learning to extract data from PDFs, matches it to matter codes in the firm’s practice‑management system, and routes approvals through a rule‑based workflow that posts directly to the firm’s accounting ledger.

name: MashaPay
description: MashaPay is a mobile‑first digital wallet built for domestic workers in the Philippines and Indonesia. It lets them receive wages, send money home, and pay everyday bills without a bank account, all through a simple, vernacular‑driven interface.
target_customer: Employers and staffing agencies pay for the service because it reduces payroll friction and guarantees on‑time wage delivery; domestic workers are the end‑users who benefit from financial inclusion and lower transaction costs.
pricing: We charge a flat 0.5 % fee on each wage deposit (capped at ₱30 / IDR 5,000) and a ₱10 per‑transaction fee for bill‑pay or cash‑out beyond the free monthly quota of 3 transactions.
traction: Since launching a private beta 4 months ago we have onboarded 3,200 workers and processed ₱12 M in wages, achieving a 92 % repeat‑use rate. Two large staffing firms have signed three‑month pilots covering 1,500 workers each, and we have a waiting list of 1,000 more.
team: Founder & CEO Aisha Ramos previously led product at a regional neobank and built a payments API used by over 2 M merchants; CTO Minh Nguyen is a former engineer at Grab with deep expertise in low‑bandwidth mobile security.
problem: Domestic workers – who make up over 15 % of the labor force in many Southeast Asian economies – are largely unbanked, receive cash wages that are easily lost or delayed, and pay high fees to informal money‑transfer agents to support their families.
solution: MashaPay gives every worker a zero‑balance e‑wallet linked to a biometric ID, enabling employers to push wages directly, workers to cash out at partner agents or via QR‑code at retail stores, and to pay utilities, airtime, and remittances instantly – all at transparent, sub‑bank fees.

name: SwitchEase
description: SwitchEase is a lightweight assistant that helps people move their primary checking account from one bank to another. We basically provide a checklist and some automated forms to cut down the hassle of closing old accounts and opening new ones.
target_customer: The product is aimed at busy professionals who are willing to pay a small fee to avoid the time spent manually transferring direct deposits, bill payments and card links.
pricing: We charge a one‑time fee of $49 per switch, with a $9 monthly subscription for ongoing alerts and updates if the user wants to keep the service active for future moves.
traction: We have a tiny beta group of about 12 users who have tried the tool and gave us generic feedback like "it was helpful"; no paid customers yet.
team: I’m the founder, a former product analyst at a regional bank who built a few internal tools for account migrations, and I’m currently working with a friend who does UI design part‑time.
problem: Switching bank accounts is still a manual, time‑consuming process: people have to find all their recurring payments, update them and make sure nothing falls through the cracks.
solution: SwitchEase walks users through every step, auto‑fills the necessary paperwork where possible, and sends reminders to update linked services, aiming to make the whole switch a weekend task instead of a week‑long nightmare.

name: FlowCart
description: FlowCart is a lightweight SaaS that pulls POS and bank data to give corner stores a daily cash‑flow forecast and simple alerts when liquidity is tight. We aim to replace the spreadsheet‑hacking most owners currently do with an app they can actually glance at on a phone.
target_customer: The paying customers are the owners or managers of independent corner stores (often a single person or a small family team) who need to avoid cash shortages that force them to turn away sales or over‑stock inventory.
pricing: We offer a freemium tier for the first 3 months, then $49 per month per store for the full forecast and alert package; a $199 per month “Enterprise” plan adds multi‑store dashboards and dedicated support.
traction: We’ve onboarded 15 stores in two Mid‑Atlantic cities, and they report a 30% drop in missed sales due to cash‑out events. We’re seeing churn of 10% over a 3‑month pilot, but the market is crowded with several well‑funded players targeting the same niche.
team: I'm Alex Patel, former VP of finance at a regional bank where I built cash‑flow models for SMBs; our CTO is Maya Liu, a data engineer who spent three years at a big‑data startup. We’re a two‑person team right now, looking to add a product designer soon.
problem: Corner stores get paid in cash, check deposits, and occasional card sales, so their cash position can swing wildly day‑to‑day. Without a reliable forecast they either run out of cash and lose sales, or keep too much idle cash on hand.
solution: FlowCart automatically syncs with the store’s POS and bank feeds, runs a rolling 7‑day cash‑flow model, and pushes a simple “green/red” signal plus a short text summary to the owner’s phone. The UI is purpose‑built for one‑hand use, so owners can check their cash health in under 30 seconds.

name: NurseNest
description: NurseNest is a digital marketplace that matches registered nurses with student‑loan refinancing options tailored to their career path and salary trajectory. We aggregate lender offers, overlay a risk‑adjusted discount, and let nurses refinance with a few clicks, saving them on interest over the life of their loans.
target_customer: Our primary customers are registered nurses (and nursing students) who carry $30‑$80K in federal or private student debt and are looking to lower their monthly payment; they pay us via a small fee and the interest spread we capture from lenders because it directly reduces their debt service cost.
pricing: We currently charge a modest origination fee that ranges from $250‑$500 per refinance and capture a 0.5‑2.0% spread on the interest rate we negotiate with partner lenders. The exact split is still being refined as we test lender appetite and regulatory constraints, so the final pricing model may evolve.
traction: In the last six months we onboarded 120 nurses, refinanced $2.3 M of principal, and received a Net Promoter Score of 78. We have soft‑letter agreements with three regional banks and a pending partnership with a national nursing association that could bring another 5,000 members onto the platform.
team: I’m Alex Rivera, former ICU RN turned fintech analyst (ex‑Goldman Sachs) and co‑founder Maya Patel, a product engineer who previously built loan‑origination software at a fintech startup. Together we combine deep clinical insight with building scalable financial products.
problem: Nurses graduate with some of the highest student‑loan balances in the country but often lack access to refinancing products that recognize their stable, high‑earning career path, leaving them stuck with high‑interest federal loans and limited cash flow.
solution: NurseNest aggregates lender data, applies a nursing‑specific risk model, and presents borrowers with pre‑qualified, lower‑rate refinance offers in a single, user‑friendly dashboard, cutting the time to refinance from weeks to days and delivering measurable interest savings.

name: AngelSyndicate
description: We built a lightweight SaaS platform that lets ultra‑small angel groups (2‑10 members) create, manage, and execute syndicated deals without the legal and admin overhead of larger tools. The app handles term‑sheet generation, pro‑rata tracking and investor communications in a single dashboard.
target_customer: Founder‑led micro‑angel clubs that pool $100k‑$500k per year and want a cheap, compliant way to syndicate deals. They pay because current solutions are either too expensive or require a full legal team.
pricing: Flat monthly subscription $199 per active syndicate + a $250 per‑deal success fee (capped at $2,500). Enterprise contracts are not offered at this stage.
traction: Beta launched 3 months ago with 4 angel clubs (total $320k syndicated). Users report a 60% reduction in admin time and have renewed for month‑2. We’ve recorded a $12k ARR run‑rate and have a waiting list of 2 more clubs.
team: Founder Alex Chen – former angel investor at AngelList and software engineer at Stripe (payments compliance). Co‑founder Maya Patel – ex‑law associate specializing in securities filings and venture financing.
problem: Micro‑angel groups spend hours drafting term sheets, tracking pro‑rata rights, and coordinating capital calls, yet existing syndication platforms are built for VC funds and cost thousands per month.
solution: Our app automates the entire syndication workflow: generate compliant term sheets with one click, track each member’s share and future rights, and send capital‑call notifications via email or Slack. The UI is intentionally simple so non‑technical angels can run deals in minutes.

name: Withholdly
description: Withholdly automates tax withholding for independent contractors and gig workers, syncing directly with payroll platforms and IRS rules in real‑time. Our SaaS platform eliminates manual calculations, prevents under‑payment penalties, and integrates with the tools freelancers already use.
target_customer: The product is sold to small‑to‑mid‑size businesses that engage a large contingent workforce (e.g., agencies, marketplaces, fintechs) and to the contractors themselves via a B2B2C partnership – they pay because it saves HR time, reduces compliance risk, and avoids costly tax penalties.
pricing: Tiered subscription: Core ($49/mo per 100 contractors), Growth ($199/mo up to 1,000 contractors) and Enterprise (custom pricing, SLA, API access). Contractors can opt‑in for a $9/mo add‑on for personal tax‑estimate dashboards.
traction: Beta launched 6 months ago with 2,300 contractors across three partner platforms; we’ve processed $1.2 M in payroll and recorded a 97% accuracy rate versus manual calculations. Early NPS is 68 and two of our pilots have already renewed for a second year, citing a 30% reduction in admin overhead.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel previously led tax compliance at Stripe and built a tax‑automation API that now serves $500M in transaction volume. CTO Luis Gomez is a former Google Cloud engineer with deep experience in real‑time data pipelines and security‑first SaaS architectures.
problem: Independent contractors are responsible for estimating and remitting their own taxes, but most lack the expertise or tools to calculate quarterly withholding accurately, leading to surprise tax bills, penalties, and wasted HR time for companies that must verify contractor compliance.
solution: Withholdly plugs into payroll APIs, pulls payment data, applies the latest IRS withholding tables and state rules, and automatically withholds the correct amount before funds are released. The platform provides real‑time dashboards, automated filing reminders, and API endpoints for partners to embed compliance directly into their checkout flows.

name: CoverCue
description: CoverCue is a platform that lets event planners tap into short‑term insurance coverage for gigs, festivals, corporate events and the like. We aim to simplify the purchase process so organisers can get protection on the fly, without a lengthy broker call.
target_customer: Event planners and small agencies who are responsible for the budget of each event and need a quick safety net in case of cancellations, liability claims or vendor failures.
pricing: We charge a flat transaction fee of roughly 5‑7% of the premium plus a small platform surcharge ($10‑$20 per policy). Premiums themselves are based on the event size and risk factors supplied by the user.
traction: We have built a prototype and a handful of beta users who have expressed interest, but we have not closed any paid policies yet and the onboarding funnel is still experimental.
team: Founded by Jenna Lee, former product manager at a mid‑size insurance carrier, and Marco Diaz, who previously built fintech tools for small businesses.
problem: Event organizers often have to source ad‑hoc insurance through brokers, which can be time‑consuming, expensive and not tailored to the short duration of most events.
solution: CoverCue digitises the underwriting workflow and offers on‑demand policies that can be bought in minutes, with automated risk inputs and instant quotes.

name: KembePay
description: KembePay is a digital remittance corridor that lets Ethiopians and Kenyans send money home in minutes, at a fraction of the cost of traditional providers. We built a lightweight web‑and‑mobile app that plugs directly into local bank APIs and uses a stable‑coin bridge for liquidity. The goal is to make cross‑border transfers as simple as sending a text.
target_customer: Primarily diaspora workers in the US, Gulf states and Europe who need a reliable, cheap way to support families in Ethiopia and Kenya; also small merchants who receive frequent cross‑border payments for inventory.
pricing: Flat fee of $0.99 per transaction plus 0.6 % of the amount, with a volume discount that drops to 0.4 % after $5,000 per month. No hidden exchange‑rate markup – we use mid‑market FX.
traction: Beta launched in Oct 2024 with 520 active users sending an aggregate $120k in the first two months. We have a pilot with two local banks in Addis Ababa and Nairobi and a partnership with a diaspora community organization that drives ~150 referrals per month. Churn is low (≈5 % monthly) but we are still far from breakeven.
team: Founder & CEO Amanuel Tadesse, ex‑Payments Engineer at PayPal and MSc in Financial Engineering from MIT; CTO Liza Mwangi, former senior developer at M-Pesa with 4 years of API integration experience. The core team is currently three engineers and one part‑time compliance officer.
problem: Sending money to Ethiopia and Kenya remains expensive (10‑12 % fees) and slow (2‑5 business days), and existing digital solutions are either blocked by local banking restrictions or lack transparent pricing. Migrants often resort to informal hawala networks, exposing them to fraud and loss.
solution: KembePay creates a regulated, end‑to‑end digital rail: users fund the app via local payment cards, we convert the fiat to a stable‑coin on a private ledger, and instantly settle with partner banks in the destination country. The architecture bypasses correspondent banks, cuts fees, and provides real‑time tracking, while compliance is handled through automated KYC/AML checks built into the onboarding flow.

name: GearCredit
description: GearCredit is a point‑of‑sale financing platform built for independent auto repair shops. We integrate directly with shop management software so mechanics can offer instant, no‑interest‑up‑to‑90‑day credit to customers at checkout. Our goal is to turn waiting for a loan approval into a seamless part of the repair estimate.
target_customer: The shops pay for the service because the financing helps them close more tickets and increase average ticket size; shop owners benefit from higher cash flow and fewer no‑shows, while their customers get quick credit to get their cars fixed.
pricing: We charge a transaction fee that is a percentage of the financed amount, plus a small flat‑fee per approved loan. The exact split (typically 3‑5% + $2‑$5) is still being tested with a few pilot shops, so we haven’t locked in a final pricing table yet.
traction: In the past 4 months we’ve onboarded 12 shops in the Chicago metro area, processed $120K in financed repairs and saw a 27% lift in average ticket size compared with their baseline. Shops report a 15% reduction in unpaid invoices and a 10% increase in repeat customers.
team: I’m Alex Martinez, former senior product manager at Square and ex‑lead engineer at a credit‑risk startup; my co‑founder Maya Patel spent 5 years building underwriting models for a regional bank and runs the risk team.
problem: Independent auto shops lose sales because many customers can’t afford large, unexpected repair bills and either delay service or walk away, leading to high idle time and revenue volatility.
solution: GearCredit embeds a quick credit decision engine into the shop’s POS, instantly approving short‑term, no‑interest loans based on the customer’s payment history and the shop’s invoice data, so the repair can start immediately and the shop gets paid upfront.

name: ShiftPay
description: ShiftPay gives hourly warehouse crews on the floor instant access to the wages they’ve already earned, without waiting for the traditional bi‑weekly payroll. We integrate directly with the W‑2 payroll system of the distribution centre and let workers withdraw a portion of earned pay via a mobile app, with the remainder automatically deposited on payday.
target_customer: The distribution centre’s HR/finance team pays for the service because it reduces turnover, improves attendance, and eliminates costly overtime penalties; the workers get the benefit for free.
pricing: We charge the employer a per‑transaction fee of $0.75 plus a small monthly platform fee of $1,500 for a 200‑employee site. For smaller pilots the fee is $0.60 per transaction and $800 per month. No fees are passed to the workers.
traction: In the past 3 months we have run a pilot with a regional Amazon‑affiliated fulfillment hub (120 active users) and saw a 15% reduction in unplanned absences and a 9% boost in overtime savings. Workers have requested the service repeatedly, and the HR director has signed an intent‑to‑purchase for a 12‑month roll‑out to three additional sites.
team: I’m Alex Rivera, former senior product manager at Paychex and a two‑year veteran of a warehouse‑automation startup; co‑founder Maya Patel built the compliance engine at a payroll SaaS that was later acquired by ADP.
problem: Warehouse workers are paid hourly, but payroll cycles are bi‑weekly, forcing many to rely on predatory payday loans or to miss shifts when cash is tight. Employers lose money on turnover and absenteeism tied to this cash‑flow gap.
solution: ShiftPay hooks into the employer’s existing payroll ledger, calculates earned but unpaid wages in real time, and lets workers request a withdrawal via an app. The amount is debited from the upcoming payroll, so the employer bears no risk, while workers get the cash they need instantly.

name: ReguChain
description: ReguChain is a SaaS platform that gives crypto exchanges an automated, real‑time compliance dashboard. It watches every trade, flags AML/KYC issues, and builds immutable audit trails so exchanges can stay ahead of regulators without hiring a team of lawyers.
target_customer: Our paying customers are the compliance officers of mid‑size to large crypto exchanges that must prove AML/KYC adherence to regulators and to their users. They buy us to reduce legal risk, avoid costly fines, and replace expensive manual monitoring processes.
pricing: We charge a base subscription of $2,000 /mo covering up to $100 M in monthly trade volume, plus $0.10 per 10,000 transactions above that. Enterprise customers can negotiate a flat‑fee model (starting at $15k /mo) that includes on‑prem deployment and dedicated support.
traction: Since launching our beta 6 months ago we’ve closed pilots with two Tier‑2 exchanges, delivering $500k ARR in recurring revenue. Both pilots reported a 92% reduction in compliance alerts that required manual review and have renewed for a second year. We’re also integrated with Chainalysis and Elliptic, and our API is live on the AWS Marketplace.
team: I’m Alex Chen, former compliance analyst at Coinbase and ex‑engineer on the AML team at JPMorgan. Co‑founder Maya Patel led product at a KYC startup that was acquired by a major crypto custodian in 2022.
problem: Crypto exchanges are under intense regulatory pressure, yet most rely on ad‑hoc spreadsheets and third‑party checks that are slow, error‑prone, and impossible to scale with growing trade volumes. A single missed flag can trigger a multi‑million‑dollar fine or a license suspension.
solution: ReguChain plugs directly into an exchange’s order‑matching engine, streams every transaction through our proprietary risk‑scoring engine, and surfaces actionable alerts in a single compliance UI. The platform automatically generates SAR‑ready reports, maintains tamper‑evident logs on a private blockchain, and lets compliance teams set custom rules that evolve with new regulator guidance.

name: CrossBorder Finance
description: CrossBorder Finance is a mobile‑first platform that tries to give recent immigrants a simple way to get basic financial advice and manage their money. We blend some AI chat features with a library of articles that we think might be useful for people new to the U.S. system.
target_customer: The product is aimed at recent immigrants who are looking for low‑cost guidance and are willing to pay a small monthly fee for a convenient tool that speaks their language.
pricing: We plan a freemium model with a $5‑$15 per month subscription for premium content and personalized chat, plus a basic free tier with limited features.
traction: So far we have a few dozen sign‑ups from a community group and some informal feedback, but no real revenue or measurable usage patterns yet.
team: I’m the founder, a former management consultant who did a couple of internships at a big bank and moved to the U.S. a few years ago; I’m currently the only full‑time member working on the product.
problem: Recent immigrants often struggle to find financial advice that’s understandable, culturally relevant, and available in their native language, leading to poor budgeting and missed opportunities.
solution: Our app offers a simple conversational interface and a curated set of resources that aim to break down basic financial concepts and help users set up things like bank accounts, credit, and budgeting in a way that feels familiar.

name: PensionPilot
description: PensionPilot is a SaaS platform that automates the whole payroll‑to‑pension workflow for small businesses, handling contributions, compliance filings and employee dashboards with a few clicks. We aim to make offering a qualified retirement plan as cheap and painless as signing up for a payroll service.
target_customer: The paying customers are owners of small enterprises (5‑50 employees) who want to attract talent but can’t afford a dedicated HR or benefits team; they pay because the automation saves them hours of manual work and reduces costly filing errors.
pricing: We charge a monthly subscription of $99 for up to 10 employees, $199 for up to 25, and custom pricing above that, plus a 0.15% per‑transaction fee on employer contributions (capped at $50 per month).
traction: We’re in beta with 12 pilots, three of which have moved to paid plans after a 4‑week trial, generating $1.2k ARR so far. Interest from a local chamber of commerce suggests demand, but churn risk is high as many prospects are also evaluating more established providers.
team: I’m Alex Rivera, former senior analyst at a large pension fund and later product lead at a payroll startup; I’m the sole full‑time founder and have recruited a part‑time compliance engineer and a freelance UI designer to help build the MVP.
problem: Small firms struggle to set up and maintain qualified pension plans because the paperwork is complex, compliance deadlines are easy to miss, and the existing solutions are either too expensive or require a dedicated HR specialist.
solution: PensionPilot connects to existing payroll software via API, auto‑generates the required contribution schedules and filing documents, and offers a simple employee portal for enrollment and tracking, effectively turning a multi‑week process into a 5‑minute monthly task.

name: FamilyFund
description: FamilyFund is a mobile‑first budgeting app built for single parents who juggle multiple responsibilities and need a quick, no‑frills way to keep their household finances on track. It pulls in all accounts, categorises spending automatically, and surfaces child‑related costs in a single view so users can plan ahead without spending hours on spreadsheets.
target_customer: The paying users are single parents – typically 30‑45 year‑olds with at least one dependent – who value a simple, time‑saving tool that helps them avoid overdrafts and plan for milestones like school fees or childcare.
pricing: We launched a 30‑day free beta and are currently experimenting with a tiered subscription: a $4.99 /month “Basic” plan that unlocks unlimited accounts and child‑expense tagging, and a $9.99 /month “Pro” plan that adds predictive cash‑flow alerts and priority support. The exact price points are still being validated with users, so we may adjust as we gather feedback.
traction: In the last 8 weeks we’ve onboarded 1,200 beta users, with a 68% weekly active rate and an average spend‑tracking accuracy of 93% versus manual entry. Early surveys show 78% would be willing to pay for the premium features, but we’re still testing the optimal price point.
team: I’m Maya Patel, former product manager at Mint and a single mother of two. I built the core algorithm during a 6‑month hackathon and am now joined by a senior engineer who spent five years at Plaid.
problem: Single parents often lack a dedicated budgeting tool that accounts for irregular income, childcare costs, and the need for rapid, on‑the‑go financial decisions, leading to missed payments and financial stress.
solution: FamilyFund syncs to all banking accounts, automatically tags child‑related expenses, and provides a week‑by‑week cash‑flow forecast that highlights upcoming bills and savings opportunities, all within a few taps.

name: PulsePay
description: PulsePay is a subscription‑billing platform built for boutique fitness studios that need a simple way to charge members for recurring classes, memberships, and add‑ons without the overhead of enterprise systems.
target_customer: Boutique gym owners (typically 1‑3 locations, 50‑300 members) who handle all their cash flow in‑house and are frustrated by the high fees and clunky integrations of generic SaaS billing tools.
pricing: We charge a flat $99 per month plus 1 % of each processed transaction (minimum $0.30 per txn). Discounts are available for studios that bundle class‑scheduling software with us.
traction: In the last 4 months we ran a pilot with 8 studios, reached $12,300 MRR and saw a 95 % renewal rate after the first three months. Studios report cutting their billing admin time by ~3 hours per week and a 0.8 % drop in payment failures versus their previous provider.
team: I’m Alex Rivera, former product manager at Stripe who built the subscription API for SMBs, and co‑founder Maya Patel, a former studio manager who ran the financial operations of three boutique gyms in NYC.
problem: Boutique gyms need to manage flexible membership plans (drop‑in, class packs, rolling contracts) while keeping transaction costs low, but most off‑the‑shelf billing platforms are either too expensive or lack the specific rules gyms use (e.g., freeze periods, class‑credit rollovers).
solution: PulsePay offers a purpose‑built billing engine that lets studio owners configure any membership cadence, apply class‑credit rules, and automate failed‑payment retries—all from a dashboard that mirrors their class‑schedule UI, with transparent per‑transaction fees instead of hidden tiered pricing.

name: PayFlow Retail
description: PayFlow Retail is a SaaS platform that lets brick‑and‑mortar retailers schedule, automate, and reconcile vendor payments in a single dashboard. We connect directly to banks, POS systems, and ERP software so merchants never miss a due date and can negotiate better terms with suppliers.
target_customer: Mid‑size retailers (100‑500 stores) that manage dozens of vendors and need to reduce accounting overhead; the CFO or head of finance purchases the solution to tighten cash flow and avoid costly late‑payment penalties.
pricing: Tiered subscription: $399/mo for up to 20 vendors, $799/mo for 20‑100 vendors, plus a 0.15% transaction fee on scheduled payments above the plan limit.
traction: Beta launched with 12 retailers controlling $4.2 M in monthly vendor spend; average time‑to‑value is 2 weeks and we’ve already saved beta users $45 k in avoided late fees in the first month. Two pilot retailers converted to paid plans after a 30‑day trial.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel previously led payments integration at Stripe and built a procurement platform acquired by Coupa. CTO Luis Gómez spent 6 years engineering real‑time settlement engines for JPMorgan.
problem: Retailers rely on spreadsheets and manual email chains to schedule vendor payments, resulting in missed due dates, late‑fee penalties, and poor cash‑flow visibility across hundreds of invoices each month.
solution: Our platform centralizes all vendor contracts, automatically triggers payments on optimal dates, and provides real‑time cash‑flow forecasting. Integration hooks pull invoice data from POS/ERP systems, and a rules engine lets finance teams set payment priorities, freeing up staff and eliminating late fees.

name: GivePay
description: GivePay is a platform that lets employees add a small charitable contribution to their payroll and routes the money to a selection of nonprofits. We aim to make the act of giving as seamless as getting paid.
target_customer: HR or finance departments at mid‑size companies that want to add a social‑impact perk for their staff; they pay for the service because it looks good for culture and retention.
pricing: We charge the employer a monthly subscription of $2‑$5 per active user, plus a small transaction fee (around 0.5%) on each donation that is passed on to the nonprofits.
traction: We've built a prototype and talked to a handful of HR managers, but we have no paying customers yet and no measurable usage data.
team: I’m the founder, a former payroll analyst who spent five years at a large HR SaaS firm. My co‑founder is a software engineer with experience in payment integrations.
problem: Employees often want to give to charity but the process is clunky—donations are done separately from payroll, requiring extra steps and paperwork.
solution: GivePay integrates a simple opt‑in on the payroll portal, automatically deducts the chosen amount each pay cycle, and aggregates the funds for a quarterly payout to the selected charities.

name: IndiSure
description: IndiSure is a digital marketplace that matches independent contractors with flexible, on‑demand insurance products from multiple carriers. We aggregate policies, surface real‑time pricing, and let users buy coverage in minutes via a single dashboard.
target_customer: Freelancers, gig workers, and small‑business owners who pay for their own insurance and need affordable, customizable coverage that can be turned on or off as their workload changes.
pricing: We charge a 5‑10% commission on each policy sold and offer a $9‑$19 monthly subscription for premium analytics and automated renewal management.
traction: Beta launched two months ago with 230 active contractors and two carrier partners; we’ve processed $12K in premium volume and have a waiting list of ~400 users, but churn is still high and competitors are moving fast.
team: Founded by Maya Patel, ex‑product manager at a major insurer, and a part‑time engineer who previously built an API gateway for a fintech startup; currently the core team is three people.
problem: Independent contractors struggle to find insurance that fits their irregular income and project‑based risk profile, often paying for bundled policies they never use or navigating a confusing, fragmented market.
solution: IndiSure centralizes dozens of carrier offerings, uses a simple risk‑profile questionnaire to recommend the right coverage, and lets users purchase, modify, or cancel policies instantly through an API‑driven platform.

name: UnionForge
description: UnionForge is a cloud‑native platform that modernises credit‑union core banking stacks without forcing a full system replacement. We wrap legacy mainframes in APIs, add modular UX components, and give branches the tools they need to launch digital products in weeks instead of years.
target_customer: Our paying customers are the credit unions themselves – specifically their operations and IT leadership – because they need a cheaper, faster path to digital services while protecting member data and regulatory compliance.
pricing: We’re still iterating on the exact model. Internally we’re experimenting with a low‑base subscription (roughly $2‑3k per month for a midsize CU) plus usage‑based fees for API calls and transaction volume, but the final tiering and caps haven’t been locked down yet.
traction: We have two pilots active – a 12‑member credit union in Ohio and a 20‑member cooperative in Texas – both reporting a 30% reduction in time‑to‑launch new products. We've also secured an LOI for a $250k seed round based on a prototype demo.
team: I’m Alex Rivera, former lead engineer at a regional bank’s digital transformation office, and co‑founder Maya Patel, who spent five years building APIs for a fintech SaaS startup.
problem: Credit unions are stuck with aging core systems that are costly to maintain and impossible to integrate with modern digital services, forcing them to either stay offline or undergo expensive, risky replacements.
solution: UnionForge layers a flexible API gateway and micro‑service toolkit over the existing core, letting unions expose account data, integrate fintech partners, and roll out mobile apps without rewriting the back‑end. It’s a plug‑and‑play upgrade path that preserves legacy investments while unlocking new revenue streams.

name: EscrowFlow
description: EscrowFlow is a SaaS platform that automates the escrow workflow for independent real‑estate agents and boutique brokerages. We digitize document collection, fund transfers, and compliance checks so agents can close deals in hours instead of days.
target_customer: Independent real‑estate agents and small brokerages (1‑10 agents) who currently juggle paper escrow packets and pay third‑party escrow officers on a per‑transaction basis. They buy because it cuts transaction time, reduces errors, and lets them keep more of their commission.
pricing: Subscription‑based tiered model: $149/month for up to 5 transactions, $299/month for up to 20, and $599/month for unlimited. No setup fee; we also offer a 30‑day free trial.
traction: Beta launched six months ago with 12 agents in the Seattle metro area. Users report a 30% faster close time and have collectively saved $8k in escrow fees. We have a wait‑list of 25 more agents and a pilot with a regional brokerage that could bring 40 additional users in Q3.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel, former product manager at a corporate escrow service and JD‑licensed attorney; CTO Luis Ortega, ex‑engineer at Plaid who built real‑time ACH pipelines.
problem: Independent agents still rely on manual, paper‑heavy escrow processes that involve multiple back‑and‑forth emails, physical signatures, and delayed fund transfers, leading to lost time, higher error rates, and lower commission capture.
solution: EscrowFlow provides a single dashboard that automatically pulls purchase agreements, sends e‑sign requests, validates buyer funds via ACH, and generates compliant escrow reports. Integrated APIs with title companies and banks eliminate manual handoffs, giving agents a clear, audit‑ready timeline for each transaction.

name: CauseCard
description: CauseCard provides a corporate‑grade payment card and expense platform built specifically for nonprofit organizations. The card integrates with the accounting software most NGOs use and automatically tags donations, grants and operational spend for transparent reporting.
target_customer: The cards are purchased by the nonprofit’s finance or development team, who need a streamlined way to spend grant money and reimburse staff while meeting donor‑imposed compliance requirements.
pricing: We charge a flat $35 /month per active card plus 0.5 % transaction fee on spend over $5,000 per month. Nonprofits under $250k annual budget get the first two cards free for the first 90 days.
traction: Since launch six months ago we have 32 NGOs on board (including two 501(c)(3)s with $10M+ annual budgets) and processed $3.2 M in spend. Our NPS is 68 and we’ve reduced average expense‑report processing time from 7 days to 1 day for beta users.
team: I’m Maya Patel, former VP of Product at a corporate card issuer and ex‑CFO of a mid‑size nonprofit. Co‑founder Luis Ramirez brings 8 years of engineering experience building compliance‑focused fintech APIs.
problem: Nonprofits juggle multiple funding streams, each with its own reporting rules, and most corporate cards are designed for for‑profit accounting. This forces staff to use ad‑hoc reimbursements, manual spreadsheets and costly bookkeeping hours, which jeopardizes donor trust.
solution: CauseCard issues a single, programmable card that can be restricted by grant, program or department. Real‑time spend tagging, automatic ledger uploads to QuickBooks/NetSuite, and built‑in audit trails give finance teams compliance‑ready data without extra manual work.

name: CommunityConnect
description: CommunityConnect is an API platform that lets small community banks expose their data to third‑party apps via open banking standards. We aim to make it easier for developers to build consumer‑facing financial services that work with local banks.
target_customer: Our primary customers are fintech developers and SaaS providers who need a simple way to plug into community bank data; the banks themselves pay a subscription for the connectivity and compliance layer.
pricing: We charge a flat monthly fee per bank (roughly $500‑$1,000) plus a per‑API‑call surcharge of $0.001‑$0.005, with volume discounts for larger integrations.
traction: We have a handful of informal conversations with a few regional banks and a couple of prototype demos, but no signed contracts or revenue yet.
team: Founder Jane Doe previously worked as a product manager at a large bank’s digital team and has a CS degree; she’s joined forces with a senior engineer who spent five years building APIs for payment processors.
problem: Community banks lack the technical resources and standardized APIs to participate in the open banking ecosystem, which forces fintechs to build custom, costly integrations for each bank.
solution: CommunityConnect provides a ready‑made, standards‑compliant API gateway that abstracts the underlying banking systems, offering banks a single integration point and developers a uniform interface to access account data, balances, and transaction history.

name: PetPayNow
description: PetPayNow is a buy‑now‑pay‑later platform built specifically for veterinary clinics. We let pet owners split expensive procedures into interest‑free installments at the point of care, while giving vets a plug‑and‑play financing widget that settles instantly to their bank account.
target_customer: The paying party is the pet owner – often a middle‑class household that wants to avoid a large upfront vet bill – and the secondary customer is the clinic, which gains higher conversion on high‑margin services.
pricing: We charge the clinic a 3% transaction fee on each financed order plus a flat $0.30 per transaction. For consumers, we offer 0% APR for up to 6 months; beyond that we apply a 12% APR on the outstanding balance.
traction: We’ve completed a 3‑month pilot with two independent practices in Austin, processing $45K in appointments and seeing a 22% lift in average ticket size. We have 150 sign‑ups on our waitlist, but conversion is still modest as we battle well‑funded BNPL incumbents moving into pet care.
team: I’m a former senior product manager at a veterinary‑software startup, and my co‑founder is an ex‑investment banking analyst who built a payments API at a regional bank. Beyond the two of us we rely on part‑time advisors; we’re actively hiring a senior engineer and a compliance lead.
problem: Veterinary care costs have risen 15% YoY, and many owners can’t afford a lump‑sum payment for procedures like surgery or dental work, leading to delayed care or lost revenue for clinics.
solution: PetPayNow embeds a BNPL widget directly into a clinic’s practice‑management system, authorizes the loan instantly, and reimburses the clinic in full on the same day. The consumer walks out with a payment plan they can manage via a mobile app, keeping cash flow smooth for both parties.

name: LedgerLoop
description: LedgerLoop is a cloud‑based accounting platform that plugs directly into a retailer’s point‑of‑sale system, syncing sales, inventory and expenses in real‑time. The goal is to give small‑to‑mid sized merchants a single dashboard for bookkeeping without the need for separate tools or manual data entry.
target_customer: The paying customers are SMB owners (restaurants, boutique stores, salons) who handle their own books or hire a part‑time bookkeeper. They need a frictionless way to reconcile daily sales with accounting, saving hours each week and reducing errors.
pricing: We’re still iterating on the pricing model. Right now we charge a modest $49/month flat fee for the core integration, plus a usage‑based surcharge (≈1 % of monthly transaction volume) for premium reconciliation features. We’re testing a tiered plan that would bundle advanced reporting and multi‑location support, but the exact price points aren’t final yet.
traction: In the past 6 months we’ve onboarded 42 merchants across three verticals, with an average of 12 % month‑over‑month revenue growth and 85 % of users staying after the first 30 days. Early feedback highlights the real‑time sync as a game‑changer, though a handful of users have asked for clearer pricing before committing to annual contracts.
team: I’m Maya Patel, former senior product manager at Square and a CPA with 8 years in retail finance. Co‑founder Luis Gomez brings engineering experience from a SaaS startup that built inventory APIs for grocery chains.
problem: SMBs using modern POS systems still have to export CSVs or manually input sales data into separate accounting software, leading to duplicate work, delayed insights, and costly bookkeeping errors.
solution: LedgerLoop embeds a lightweight SDK into existing POS platforms, automatically categorizing transactions, reconciling them against bank feeds, and generating ready‑to‑file financial statements—all visible in a single web dashboard.

name: BorderBoutique Pay
description: BorderBoutique Pay is a lightweight, API‑first platform that lets boutique fashion e‑commerce brands accept payments from U.S. shoppers in euros and vice‑versa, handling currency conversion, local tax compliance and settlement in under 48 hours. We focus on a single vertical – small‑batch luxury apparel – to keep the integration simple and the cost low.
target_customer: The paying customer is the boutique brand itself – typically a 2‑person studio that sells on its own site and wants to avoid the 3‑5% fees and paperwork of traditional merchant acquirers when going cross‑border.
pricing: We charge a flat $0.30 per transaction plus 1.2% of the converted amount, with a $99 monthly minimum for volumes under $5k. No‑commitment, no‑setup‑fee plan for early adopters.
traction: In the past 8 weeks we have onboarded three brands (total $27k processed) and completed a pilot with a fourth that will launch next month. Our pilot users report a 30% reduction in checkout abandonment on foreign‑currency pages.
team: I'm Alex Liu, former payments engineer at Stripe and co‑founder of a boutique Shopify plugin that hit $200k ARR before being acquired. My co‑founder Maya Patel ran compliance at a EU‑based fintech and built the KYC pipeline we now reuse.
problem: Small luxury e‑commerce brands can’t afford the complex, high‑margin solutions that big retailers use, yet they still need to convert USD payments to EUR, collect VAT, and settle quickly to keep cash flow healthy.
solution: Our API wraps a single FX partner, automates VAT calculation, and deposits the net euros into the brand’s local bank account, all while providing a one‑page checkout that feels native to the shopper. Because we only support one niche vertical, we can keep the tech stack lean and the fees transparent.

name: RuralVault
description: RuralVault is a mobile‑first digital bank built for farmers, merchants, and households in underserved rural regions. We provide a full‑service account, low‑cost credit, and cash‑less payments, all optimized for low‑bandwidth networks and local languages.
target_customer: Our primary customers are unbanked adults in villages (farmers, small traders, and gig workers) who pay for a free basic account and a small monthly fee for premium features; they value security, instant payments, and access to micro‑loans to smooth seasonal cash flow.
pricing: Basic account: free; Premium package (advanced analytics, higher withdrawal limits, priority loan processing): $1.50 / month. Transaction fee: 0.5 % on merchant payments, 0.75 % on peer‑to‑peer transfers. Micro‑loan interest starts at 4 % p.a. for the first $500.
traction: In the past six months we have onboarded 12,300 active users across three pilot districts, processed $2.1 M in transactions and disbursed $850 k in micro‑loans with a 96 % repayment rate. Partner banks have pledged $1.2 M in liquidity and we are currently in talks with two regional telecoms to bundle our service with their data plans.
team: I’m Ananya Patel, former product lead at a Tier‑1 neobank where I launched two digital savings products, and co‑founder Luis Gomez, a former agritech CTO who built the payments stack for a $150 M SaaS startup serving cooperatives.
problem: Rural households lack affordable, safe banking: they rely on cash, travel hours to the nearest branch, and face predatory money‑lenders. Low connectivity and language barriers make existing digital banks unusable, while cash‑based transactions are inefficient and insecure.
solution: RuralVault delivers a lightweight Android app that works on 2G networks, supports regional dialects, and syncs offline transactions when connectivity returns. We partner with local agents to provide cash‑in/out via POS devices, and we use alternative data (mobile usage, satellite weather) to underwrite micro‑loans, enabling instant, low‑cost credit without collateral.

name: TaxifyAI
