description: We build a cloud‑based service that automatically calculates, collects and remits sales tax for online merchants. Our platform plugs into most e‑commerce carts and promises to keep businesses compliant with minimal effort.
target_customer: Small‑to‑medium e‑commerce brands that sell across multiple US states and need a simple way to handle sales tax without hiring a specialist.
pricing: Subscription‑based, starting at $49/month for up to $5K monthly sales volume, with higher tiers for larger merchants (e.g., $199/month for up to $50K).
traction: We have a handful of beta users and a waiting list of a few dozen sign‑ups, but no paying customers yet and no concrete revenue metrics.
team: Founder John Doe, former software engineer at a large marketplace platform; co‑founder Maya Patel, previously a consultant on tax compliance for e‑commerce clients.
problem: Online sellers struggle to keep up with constantly changing sales‑tax rules across states, leading to compliance risk and manual overhead.
solution: Our API pulls the latest tax rates, applies them to each transaction in real time, and can generate filing reports for each jurisdiction, reducing the need for manual calculations.

name: HealCollect
description: HealCollect is a cloud‑native platform that modernises health‑care debt collection with AI‑driven outreach, real‑time compliance monitoring and integrated payment options. We aim to cut manual workflow, improve patient experience and raise recovery rates for providers.
target_customer: Hospital finance departments and large physician groups that own the receivables; they pay because faster, compliant collections reduce write‑offs and avoid costly attorney fees.
pricing: A SaaS subscription from $2,500 /mo for up to $150k in monthly receivables plus a 1.2% success‑fee on recovered balances (capped at $5k per month). Custom enterprise pricing available for larger networks.
traction: Beta launched Q1 2025 with a regional hospital system (2 M in outstanding balances) – we’ve recovered $180k in the first 8 weeks and reduced manual effort by 40%. Early conversations with three additional health‑systems are in the pipeline.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel spent 6 years at a big‑four advisory practice building payment‑technology solutions for insurers; CTO Jason Liu is a former software lead at a health‑tech startup and currently works part‑time while we raise seed capital.
problem: Healthcare providers still rely on legacy, paper‑based collection processes that are slow, error‑prone and often run afoul of HIPAA and state debt‑collection regulations, leading to high write‑offs and patient dissatisfaction.
solution: HealCollect automates the entire outreach cycle – from AI‑segmented patient communication (email, SMS, voice) to secure online payment portals and real‑time compliance checks – giving providers a single dashboard to track, manage and recover outstanding balances efficiently.

name: GoalReward
description: GoalReward lets consumers link their personal savings goals to a loyalty‑points ecosystem. Every time a user hits a milestone—like saving $100 or staying on track for a month—they earn redeemable points from partner merchants, turning boring budgeting into a game.
target_customer: We sell the platform to banks and neobanks that want to boost user stickiness, and to retailers looking for a new acquisition channel. End‑users get the benefit for free, but the financial institutions and merchants pay for the integration.
pricing: We’re still solidifying the model, but the idea is a low‑base monthly fee per active user (roughly $0.50‑$1.00) plus a small transaction‑share on redeemed points. We also plan a revenue‑share tier for high‑volume partners, though the exact percentages are TBD.
traction: In the past six months we’ve run a closed beta with two regional banks and a boutique retailer, driving a 12% lift in weekly savings deposits among 1,200 test users. Our API has processed $45k in virtual points, and both partners have expressed interest in a full rollout.
team: I’m Maya Patel, former product lead at a top‑10 US bank where I built a rewards program that grew deposits by 8%. My co‑founder, Luis Ortega, is a seasoned engineer from a fintech startup that exited to a major payment processor.
problem: People know they should save, but traditional savings accounts offer no immediate gratification, so motivation wanes and deposits stay flat.
solution: GoalReward injects instant, tangible rewards into the savings journey by converting progress into points that can be spent at everyday merchants, creating a feedback loop that keeps users engaged and nudges them toward their financial goals.

name: HarvestPay
description: HarvestPay is a white‑label API that gives niche online farmer‑market platforms the ability to push funds to their sellers the instant they make a sale. We handle the banking integration, compliance and risk‑engine so the marketplace can focus on users, not payouts.
target_customer: We sell to the marketplace operators (e.g., Farm2Table, GreenPatch) who are willing to pay for a better seller experience because it drives higher retention and transaction volume.
pricing: We charge a flat $0.25 per payout plus 0.5% of the transferred amount; for marketplaces under $100k monthly volume we offer a $199/month starter tier with a bulk discount after $50k volume.
traction: Beta launched with Farm2Table in March – 120 of their 150 sellers have opted in, and average payout speed has gone from 3‑5 business days to under 2 minutes. The marketplace reports a 12% lift in weekly active sellers since integration.
team: Founder Alex Liu spent 5 years building payments infrastructure at Stripe and 2 years running the finance team at a regional food‑hub marketplace.
problem: Small‑scale marketplace sellers (artisan growers, boutique producers) often wait days for cash, which hurts inventory replenishment and makes them vulnerable to larger platforms that promise instant payouts.
solution: HarvestPay plugs directly into the marketplace’s checkout flow, instantly settles the seller’s bank account via our partner bank network, and provides real‑time compliance checks, so sellers get paid the second a buyer’s card is authorized.

name: AirPulse Health
description: AirPulse Health builds a home‑based remote monitoring platform for COPD patients that continuously tracks lung function, oxygen saturation, and activity levels, then alerts clinicians to early signs of exacerbation. Our AI‑driven dashboard gives care teams actionable insights without adding paperwork, while patients get a simple mobile app that guides them through daily self‑checks.
target_customer: Hospitals and integrated health systems pay for the service because it reduces COPD readmissions and improves quality metrics; insurers also co‑fund because every avoided admission saves $15‑20k on average.
pricing: Subscription‑based model: $199 per patient per month for the full sensor‑plus‑software bundle, with volume discounts (e.g., 10% off for >500 patients). A separate $49/month add‑on for advanced AI‑risk scoring is optional.
traction: Pilot with two regional health systems (MetroHealth and Evergreen Care) covering 1,200 patients has yielded a 30% reduction in 30‑day COPD readmissions and a Net Promoter Score of 78 among clinicians. We have secured a $1.2 M seed round and are on track to onboard 3 additional hospitals by Q4 2026.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, former pulmonology fellow turned CTO with a PhD in biomedical signal processing; co‑founder Ryan Chen is a former product lead at a tele‑health startup and built the FDA‑cleared sensor hardware used in our platform.
problem: COPD exacerbations are unpredictable; 20% of patients end up in the emergency department each year, costing the system $50 B annually. Current home monitoring is fragmented, often manual, and fails to provide clinicians with timely, actionable data.
solution: AirPulse equips patients with a single, FDA‑cleared sensor that measures spirometry, SpO₂, and activity 24/7. Data streams securely to our AI platform, which flags deteriorations 48‑72 hours before they become clinical emergencies and routes alerts directly to the care team’s EHR, enabling pre‑emptive interventions.

name: CampusCare
description: CampusCare is a web‑based platform that offers a quick mental‑health check‑in for students and routes them to the right resources on campus. We aim to make it easier for universities to spot students who might need help before a crisis hits.
target_customer: University counseling centers (or the university administration) – they would pay because they need a simple way to manage the growing demand for mental‑health support without hiring more staff.
pricing: Flat subscription per active student, roughly $0.30‑$0.50 per user per month, with a minimum of $2,000 a month for institutions of 5k+ students.
traction: We ran a small pilot at a single campus last semester and got informal feedback; no formal usage metrics or revenue yet.
team: I’m a former psychology graduate who spent two years at a mental‑health startup, and my co‑founder is a software engineer from a collegiate app project.
problem: Colleges are seeing a surge in student mental‑health issues but their counseling services are overwhelmed and lack a low‑friction way to triage incoming concerns.
solution: A short, AI‑guided questionnaire that students can complete on their phones; the system flags high‑risk responses and sends alerts to campus counselors while providing low‑risk students with self‑help resources.

name: NephroSync
description: NephroSync is a cloud‑based care‑coordination platform that links dialysis clinics, nephrologists, and home‑care providers in real time. It aggregates treatment schedules, labs, and patient-reported outcomes into a single dashboard to reduce missed appointments and hospitalizations.
target_customer: Dialysis providers (clinic groups & hospital networks) pay for the service because it lowers operational costs and improves quality metrics that affect reimbursement.
pricing: Subscription per active patient seat – $12 / patient / month after a 3‑month free pilot; volume discounts for networks >200 patients.
traction: Pilot with two mid‑size renal clinics (≈350 patients) showed a 15% drop in missed dialysis sessions and a 10% increase in documented care plans; we’re now in talks with two regional health systems.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel, MD, spent 6 years as a nephrology fellow running a dialysis unit; CTO Daniel Kim, ex‑Google Cloud engineer, joined 4 months ago.
problem: Dialysis patients often fall through the cracks because their care involves multiple providers who use disparate EMR systems, leading to scheduling errors, duplicated labs, and preventable readmissions.
solution: NephroSync integrates with existing EMRs via HL7/FHIR, auto‑syncs appointment calendars, flags lab results out of range, and lets clinicians exchange secure messages—all from a single, mobile‑friendly interface.

name: DocScribeAI
description: DocScribeAI builds an AI‑powered scribe that sits inside a solo family practice’s EMR and turns the doctor’s spoken notes into structured chart entries in real time. The goal is to give one‑person clinics back the time they lose to documentation without hiring a human scribe.
target_customer: The paying customer is the solo family practice physician (or the small clinic owner) who wants to cut down on after‑hours charting and keep more of the reimbursement for their time.
pricing: We’re testing a subscription model that could sit somewhere between $149‑$299 per month per physician, with a possible per‑note usage add‑on, but we haven’t locked down the exact tiers yet.
traction: We’ve run a 3‑month pilot with 12 doctors, who report a 20% reduction in admin time and a 15% increase in patient slots. Two of those practices have signed LOIs for a 6‑month rollout.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, a former family physician who spent 5 years building EMR integrations at a large health system, and co‑founder Alex Chen, who led AI product development at a med‑tech startup.
problem: Solo family doctors spend up to 30% of their workday on documentation, which erodes patient‑facing time and forces many to work evenings to finish charts.
solution: Our AI listens to the doctor’s dictation during the visit, auto‑populates the EMR fields, and surfaces a quick “confirm‑or‑edit” screen, letting clinicians finalize notes in under a minute.

name: AuthFlow AI
description: AuthFlow AI builds a lightweight, AI‑driven bot that plugs into a single insurer’s legacy prior‑authorization platform and auto‑fills, routes, and validates paperwork in real time. The goal is to shave hours off every claim review for that insurer’s behavioral‑health line‑of‑business.
target_customer: The product is sold to the Chief Digital Officer of a regional health insurer (≈150k members) who wants to cut manual PA labor costs in their behavioral‑health division and meet state‑mandated turnaround times.
pricing: We charge a per‑claim automation fee of $0.05–$0.08, with a minimum monthly commitment of $7,500 (≈150k automated claims). A one‑time integration fee of $12k covers the custom connector to the insurer’s legacy system.
traction: In the past 3 months we piloted the bot on 12,000 behavioral‑health PA requests for HealthFirst Medicaid, achieving a 42% reduction in processing time and a 15% drop in claim rejections. The insurer has signed an LOI for a 12‑month pilot covering up to 200k claims.
team: I’m a former senior analyst at a national PBM where I built claim‑validation tools, and my co‑founder is a PhD in NLP who spent five years at a health‑AI startup that exited to a major EHR vendor.
problem: Insurers still rely on handwritten PDFs and phone calls to approve behavioral‑health services, resulting in average turnaround times of 5‑7 business days, missed deadlines, and high labor costs.
solution: Our AI bot reads the physician’s note, extracts CPT codes, verifies coverage rules, and posts a completed authorization request straight into the insurer’s portal, prompting only a human review when the model is uncertain.

name: Infuso
description: Infuso is a SaaS platform that connects patients, home nurses and infusion pharmacies to automate scheduling, dosage verification and remote monitoring of home infusion therapy. The system plugs into existing smart pumps and EMRs to give clinicians real‑time adherence data and alerts.
target_customer: Our primary buyers are health systems and specialty infusion centers that reimburse home infusion services; they pay because the platform reduces complications, readmissions and nursing overhead. Secondary payers are large commercial insurers that see cost savings from higher adherence.
pricing: We charge a $149/month base fee per infusion site plus $9 per active patient per month. Enterprise contracts for >500 patients get volume discounts (down to $5/patient). No upfront hardware cost – we integrate with existing pumps via API.
traction: In Q1‑2024 we completed a 6‑month pilot with two regional health systems serving 212 home‑infusion patients. Adherence rose from 78% to 94% and adverse‑event calls dropped 42%, saving an estimated $1.2M in avoided ER visits. The pilots generated $68k ARR and two letters of intent for a $1.5M rollout in H2‑2024.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel is a former RN infusion specialist who spent 5 years building clinical decision support tools at a med‑tech startup. CTO Luis Romero earned his PhD in biomedical engineering and previously led SDK development for a FDA‑cleared smart‑pump platform.
problem: Home infusion therapy is plagued by missed doses, dosage errors and fragmented communication between patients, nurses and pharmacies, leading to costly complications and wasteful nurse trips.
solution: Infuso synchronizes smart pump data with a secure cloud dashboard, auto‑generates dosing schedules, sends daily reminders to patients, and alerts nurses and pharmacists to deviations. The care‑team portal provides real‑time compliance metrics, enabling proactive interventions before a complication occurs.

name: NutriBari
description: NutriBari is a mobile platform that lets bariatric surgery patients log food, water and supplement intake. It aims to give clinicians a quick view of how well patients are following their post‑op nutrition plans.
target_customer: The primary buyers are bariatric clinics and surgery centers that want to improve post‑operative outcomes; they would pay for the service to reduce complications and improve patient satisfaction.
pricing: We charge a per‑patient subscription – roughly $9 / month for the basic tracking suite and $19 / month for the clinician‑dashboard add‑on.
traction: We have a very small pilot running at one hospital with about 5 patients and a handful of clinicians testing the dashboard. No revenue yet and we’re still working out the core workflow.
team: I’m a former dietitian who worked in a bariatric program for five years and a software engineer who built a few health‑related apps. We teamed up last year to start NutriBari.
problem: After bariatric surgery, patients often struggle to meet the strict protein, vitamin and fluid guidelines, leading to malnutrition and readmissions.
solution: Our app offers simple food‑logging templates, reminders for supplements and a dashboard that flags deviations so clinicians can intervene early.

name: EaseAge
description: EaseAge is a mobile‑first platform that combines evidence‑based physical‑therapy videos, AI‑driven activity analytics, and a peer‑support community to help adults over 60 manage chronic musculoskeletal pain at home. We aim to keep users moving without relying on opioids or frequent clinic visits.
target_customer: The primary payer is Medicare Advantage plans and private insurers, because the solution reduces costly specialist visits and hospital readmissions; secondary customers are the seniors themselves who can purchase a subsidized subscription for out‑of‑pocket coverage.
pricing: We charge a per‑member‑per‑month (PMPM) license to insurers ranging from $12‑$18 depending on enrollment size. For direct‑to‑consumer users we offer a $9.99 monthly subscription with a discounted $79 yearly rate.
traction: In the past six months we have onboarded two small Medicare Advantage pilots covering ~1,200 members, seeing a 15% reduction in reported pain scores and a 10% drop in physical‑therapy referrals. We have also collected 5,000+ video views and a 30% weekly active user rate, but we have yet to break past the pilot stage.
team: I’m a former physiatrist‑researcher turned engineer (MD, Stanford PhD) who spent 8 years building digital rehab tools, and I’ve partnered with a senior data scientist (ex‑Google Health) who handles the AI pipeline. Apart from us, we have a part‑time UX designer and an advisory board of two retired clinicians.
problem: A growing share of the U.S. population over 65 lives with chronic back, knee, or joint pain, yet the current care model is fragmented, heavily reliant on in‑person therapy, and costly for insurers.
solution: Our app delivers personalized, low‑impact exercise programs that adapt in real time to sensor data from smartphones or wearables, nudges users to stay active, and links them to a moderated community for accountability, all while feeding de‑identified outcomes back to payers for value‑based reimbursement.

name: DentAI Rural
description: We’ve built a cloud‑based AI that reads bite‑wing and panoramic X‑rays in seconds, flagging cavities, periodontal disease and early lesions for dentists who don’t have a radiology specialist on staff. The system runs on low‑cost hardware that most rural clinics already have.
target_customer: The paying customer is the clinic owner or the regional health authority that funds primary‑care dental services; they need to cut referral costs and improve diagnostic accuracy for their patient base.
pricing: We’re still figuring out the optimal model – currently we’re running a pilot with a flat $150‑per‑month subscription that includes up to 500 scans, plus a $0.25 per‑extra‑scan fee. Some clinics prefer a pure per‑scan price, so we’re testing $0.30‑$0.40 per image as an alternative. Pricing is being refined based on feedback.
traction: In the last 4 months we’ve signed up 5 pilot clinics across three states, processed >2,000 images, and the dentists report a 30% reduction in referrals to specialists. Our AI is hitting 92% sensitivity on cavity detection in real‑world data, which is encouraging but still early.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, a DDS who grew up in a rural county and spent the last 3 years building AI models at a university lab; co‑founder Raj Singh is a former Google Cloud engineer who led medical‑image pipelines.
problem: Rural dental offices often lack on‑site radiology expertise, forcing costly referrals and leading to missed early‑stage disease, which harms patients and inflates healthcare spending.
solution: Our AI plugs into any standard X‑ray viewer, runs locally or in a low‑bandwidth cloud, and delivers a visual heat‑map and confidence score instantly, so the dentist can make a more informed diagnosis without sending images out.

name: TrialFit Rare
description: TrialFit Rare is an AI‑driven matchmaking platform that connects ultra‑orphan patient cohorts with the few clinical trials that actually need them. We pull data from specialty EMRs, patient registries and advocacy group registries to surface eligible participants in real time.
target_customer: Our paying customers are boutique biotech firms and pharma divisions that sponsor rare‑disease trials – they need a reliable way to fill tiny enrolment windows without spending weeks on manual chart review.
pricing: We charge a flat subscription of $4,500 per month per trial, plus a $1,200 per confirmed enrolment bonus. Early‑stage pilots get a $2,000 discount for the first three months.
traction: In Q1 we ran a pilot with two orphan‑drug companies (one for Batten disease, one for familial amyloid polyneuropathy). The platform identified 38 potential participants, led to 10 confirmed matches and 2 actual enrolments within six weeks. Both sponsors are now negotiating a 12‑month rollout.
team: Founder Dr. Maya Patel is a former clinical pharmacologist at a rare‑disease CRO and holds a PhD in biomedical informatics; she previously built a patient‑registry platform that was acquired by a major EHR vendor.
problem: Rare‑disease trials often fail to recruit because eligible patients are scattered across multiple hospitals, registries and advocacy groups, and investigators lack a single view of that fragmented data.
solution: Our platform aggregates de‑identified clinical and registry data, applies disease‑specific eligibility algorithms, and pushes match alerts to trial sites and patient advocacy groups, dramatically cutting the time from screening to enrolment.

name: RxAlign
description: RxAlign is a cloud‑native pharmacy benefit platform built for self‑insured employers. We aggregate prescription data, negotiate tiered pricing in real time, and deliver a transparent, employee‑first formulary that reduces waste and drives down drug spend without sacrificing care.
target_customer: The buyer is the employer's benefits or HR team (or CFO) who pays the pharmacy spend directly; they need a predictable, lower‑cost drug benefit that keeps talent happy and avoids surprise claims.
pricing: We charge a flat‑fee per covered employee per month ($5‑$12 depending on volume) plus a modest transaction fee (0.5% of total pharmacy spend) that aligns incentives with cost savings.
traction: Pilot with two Fortune 500 self‑insured employers covering 3,200 employees has already generated $1.2 M in annualized savings and a 13% reduction in out‑of‑pocket spend for employees. Our API is live, we’ve processed 45,000 Rx claims in the last 90 days, and three mid‑size employers are in LOI stage.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel (ex‑Pharmacy Benefits Manager at UnitedHealth, MBA MIT) and CTO Luis Ramirez (former lead engineer at Synthea Health, built scaling data pipelines for 10+ PBM platforms).
problem: Self‑insured employers face opaque pharmacy pricing, high administrative overhead, and rising specialty drug costs, leading to unpredictable budgets and dissatisfied employees who see large co‑pays.
solution: RxAlign centralizes claim data, applies AI‑driven formulary optimization, and negotiates real‑time discounts with pharmacies and manufacturers. Employers get a single dashboard, employees receive personalized cost‑saving recommendations, and the whole system cuts administrative friction and drug spend by up to 15%.

name: KidTherapy
description: KidTherapy is a cloud‑based platform that helps pediatric therapy clinics manage their appointment schedules and communicate with families. We aim to make booking and coordinating sessions a little easier for everyone involved.
target_customer: The primary buyers are clinic administrators and practice owners who need to streamline scheduling and reduce phone tag with parents, while parents benefit from more flexible booking options.
pricing: We plan a subscription model: $99 per month for small clinics (up to 5 therapists) and $199 per month for larger practices, with a free trial for the first 30 days.
traction: We have a prototype and have been demoing it to a handful of local pediatric therapy centers, but we have not closed any paying customers yet. Early feedback is mostly about wanting more features.
team: I’m Alex Rivera, a former pediatric PT who spent five years managing clinic operations and a brief stint in a health‑tech startup. My co‑founder, Maya Patel, has a background in software development and UI design.
problem: Therapy clinics spend a lot of time on manual scheduling, phone calls, and paperwork, which leads to missed appointments and frustrated families.
solution: Our platform provides a simple calendar interface, automated reminders, and a parent portal where families can view and request slots, aiming to reduce administrative overhead.

name: OncoSecond Opinion
description: We provide a web‑based platform that connects cancer patients with board‑certified oncologists for remote, evidence‑based second opinions. The service aggregates the patient's records, runs a quick AI‑assisted review, and schedules a video consult with a specialist who can confirm or revise the original treatment plan.
target_customer: Patients (or their families) who are already paying for cancer care, and the insurers/TPAs that want to reduce unnecessary treatments. The value proposition is clear – a second opinion can prevent costly, ineffective therapies and give patients peace of mind.
pricing: Per‑case fee of $250 for a standard second‑opinion package (record upload, AI triage, 30‑minute video consult). Additional $100 for each extra consult or expedited review. We also offer a bulk contract for insurers at $150 per case at volume.
traction: In the past 3 months we ran a pilot with a regional cancer centre: 30 patients booked, 21 (70%) said they would use the service again and 4 clinicians have already shifted a treatment plan after our review. We have a wait‑list of 50 patients and a letter of intent from one mid‑size insurer for a 6‑month pilot.
team: I (Dr. Maya Patel) am a board‑certified medical oncologist with 8 years of clinical practice and a stint as a health‑policy analyst at a payer. Our CTO, Alex Liu, built AI pipelines for a tele‑health startup and left after a 12‑month stint to join full‑time. We currently have no dedicated product manager or sales lead.
problem: Cancer patients often receive a treatment recommendation that is hard to verify, leading to over‑treatment, unnecessary side‑effects, and high out‑of‑pocket costs. Access to a trusted, timely second opinion is limited, especially for patients outside major academic centers.
solution: Our platform streamlines the whole workflow: patients upload imaging and pathology reports, our AI engine flags key data, and a vetted network of oncologists reviews the case and delivers a concise written opinion plus a video call. The result is a faster, lower‑cost alternative to the traditional referral model.

name: SomniLift
description: We’ve built a clinically‑validated digital therapeutic that uses CBT‑I, adaptive sleep coaching, and personalized soundscapes to help adults fall asleep faster and stay asleep longer. The app runs on a smartphone and integrates with wearables to close the feedback loop in real time.
target_customer: Employers and health insurers buy the program for their covered employees because better sleep drives productivity and reduces downstream healthcare costs; we also sell directly to tech‑savvy individuals who are willing to pay out‑of‑pocket for a proven insomnia solution.
pricing: We’re still iterating on the pricing structure. The current draft includes a B2B per‑member‑per‑month tier at $9‑$12 and a consumer subscription at $14‑$19 per month, with a discounted annual plan, but we haven’t locked in the exact mix of usage‑based vs flat fees yet.
traction: In the past six months we’ve enrolled 2,200 users, 68% of whom report a ≥30‑minute reduction in sleep latency after four weeks, and three midsize employers have piloted the product with a Net Promoter Score of 62. We’re also in talks with two insurers for a pilot that could reach 10k lives.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, a former sleep‑medicine fellow turned product engineer, and I co‑founded the company with Alex Chen, a software architect who spent five years building AI‑driven health platforms at a large EHR vendor.
problem: Insomnia affects roughly 30% of adults, yet most patients never receive evidence‑based therapy because access to trained CBT‑I clinicians is limited and pharmaceuticals have side‑effects.
solution: SomniLift delivers a fully digital CBT‑I program that tailors content to each user’s sleep data, provides daily coaching nudges, and replaces the need for weekly in‑person sessions, making high‑quality insomnia treatment scalable and affordable.

name: MaternitiQ
description: MaternitiQ is a SaaS platform that streams biometric data from FDA‑cleared wearables into a real‑time risk dashboard for obstetricians managing high‑risk pregnancies. The system flags pre‑eclampsia, fetal distress, and preterm‑labor signatures before they become emergencies.
target_customer: Hospital obstetrics departments and boutique high‑risk pregnancy clinics – they pay because the platform reduces ICU transfers, shortens LOS, and improves outcomes that affect both quality scores and reimbursements.
pricing: We charge a per‑patient license: $199/month for the dashboard + device lease, with a volume discount for >100 patients (≈$149/month). A $5,000 onboarding fee covers integration and staff training.
traction: Pilot trials at two academic medical centers (total 38 patients) showed a 30% reduction in emergency transfers and earned us a $150k research grant. We have IRB clearance and a provisional CLIA waiver, but revenue is still pre‑seed.
team: I’m Dr. Anika Patel, MD (maternal‑fetal medicine) turned entrepreneur; previously led a tele‑monitoring team at a NICU startup. Co‑founder Sam Liu, PhD, built the signal‑processing engine for a wearable cardiac monitor used in 200,000 patients.
problem: High‑risk pregnant people often receive only intermittent clinic visits; sudden complications like pre‑eclampsia can develop between appointments, leading to costly emergency interventions and worse outcomes.
solution: MaternitiQ equips patients with a discreet, continuous wrist sensor that streams heart‑rate variability, blood pressure, and uterine activity to a cloud analytics engine. Clinicians see an intuitive risk score and receive automated alerts, enabling proactive interventions while keeping patients at home.

name: NavMed Care
description: NavMed Care is a mobile‑first platform that gives Medicaid members a personal care‑navigator powered by AI and a network of community health workers. It centralizes eligibility, appointment scheduling, transportation, and social‑service referrals so users can actually get the care they’re entitled to.
target_customer: State Medicaid agencies and Managed Care Organizations pay for the service because it reduces costly emergency visits, improves quality metrics, and helps them meet federal accountability goals.
pricing: We charge a flat per‑member‑per‑month fee of $3‑$5 (tiered by enrollment volume) plus a performance‑based bonus for each 10% reduction in avoidable ER visits.
traction: In Q1‑2026 we launched pilots with two state Medicaid programs covering 5,200 members. Early data show a 68% drop in missed primary‑care appointments and a 22% decline in preventable ER use, earning us a $250k grant and a letter of intent for a $2M statewide rollout.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, former CMS health‑policy analyst who built a tele‑triage product at a Fortune‑10 health system; my co‑founder, Carlos Ruiz, spent 7 years scaling community‑health worker programs for a large nonprofit. Both have deep relationships with state Medicaid officials.
problem: Medicaid members often lack a single point of contact to navigate fragmented benefits, leading to missed appointments, unnecessary ER visits, and higher costs for the state.
solution: NavMed Care assigns each member a digital navigator that syncs eligibility data, automatically books appointments, coordinates transportation, and alerts a human community health worker when social‑determinant issues arise, closing the loop between insurance and care delivery.

name: FlexiFit Health
description: FlexiFit Health is building an online platform that connects employees with physical therapists who specialize in musculoskeletal issues. The goal is to make it easy for companies to offer remote PT services as part of their wellness benefits.
target_customer: We sell to midsize employers (200‑2,000 employees) who want to add low‑cost injury‑prevention and rehab perks for their workforce.
pricing: Subscription‑based model: roughly $5‑$8 per employee per month, with a small per‑session fee for any on‑demand appointments.
traction: We have a pilot with one anonymous client that started a couple of months ago, but we have not yet collected usage data or signed any contracts beyond the pilot.
team: Founder Alex Rivera, former corporate wellness manager and PT assistant, with a background in health‑tech consulting.
problem: Companies face rising costs from musculoskeletal injuries and low engagement with traditional in‑person PT programs, leading to absenteeism and decreased productivity.
solution: Our platform offers a simple web portal where employees can book virtual PT sessions, receive personalized exercise plans, and track progress, aiming to lower injury‑related costs and improve wellbeing.

name: ClearPath Discharge
description: ClearPath is a SaaS platform that coordinates every step of a patient’s discharge—from medication reconciliation to home‑care scheduling—directly inside the EHR. By turning the discharge checklist into a real‑time, assignable workflow, we aim to cut avoidable readmissions without adding to clinicians’ workload.
target_customer: Hospital discharge teams (case managers, nurses, physicians) who are ultimately funded by the health system’s finance office because reduced readmissions improve both quality metrics and the bottom line.
pricing: Subscription‑based pricing per licensed bed: $1,800 / month for 0–100 beds, $3,200 / month for 101–250 beds, plus a $0.15 per discharge add‑on for hospitals that need extra analytics. Annual contracts with a 12‑month minimum.
traction: We’ve run a 12‑week pilot at two midsize community hospitals (total 350 discharges) and saw a 10 % drop in 30‑day readmission rates. Users report faster task completion, but we’re still gathering quantitative data and have not yet closed a paid contract.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, former hospital operations director turned engineer (M.S. in Health Informatics, 8 years in discharge planning). The technical side is handled by our CTO, Alex Chen, a former Amazon AWS engineer who left his job last year to join full‑time. We currently have a single product designer on contract and rely on advisory board volunteers for clinical input.
problem: Hospital discharge is a fragmented process: paperwork lives in multiple systems, tasks slip through the cracks, and delays often lead to avoidable readmissions that cost hospitals millions and hurt their quality scores.
solution: ClearPath stitches the EHR, pharmacy, and home‑care portals together into one configurable workflow board. Clinicians get automated task assignments, real‑time status dashboards, and AI‑driven alerts for missing steps, so the discharge plan stays complete and on schedule.

name: SepsiSense
description: SepsiSense is an AI‑powered platform that ingests continuous vitals, lab results and EMR data to flag sepsis risk in ICU patients the moment physiological patterns diverge from normal. Our prototype already runs in two academic hospitals and has shown a 30% reduction in time‑to‑antibiotics.
target_customer: Hospital systems and ICU directors – they pay because early sepsis detection directly improves outcomes, reduces ICU length of stay, and avoids costly penalties from Medicare quality metrics.
pricing: We are still hammering out the exact model. The plan is to charge either a per‑bed subscription (roughly $1,500‑$2,500 / bed / month) or a per‑alert fee (about $30‑$50 per validated sepsis flag). At this stage we are piloting both structures with our early partners and will lock in pricing after we quantify the ROI for each hospital.
traction: Pilot deployments at Mercy Hospital and Stanford Health have processed >10,000 patient‑hours, yielding 85% sensitivity and 78% specificity. Both sites have reported a 0.7‑day reduction in ICU average length of stay. We have a Letter of Intent from a regional health network covering 12 ICUs, and we are in talks with a major EHR vendor for integration.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, former critical‑care fellow turned data scientist (PhD, MIT). Co‑founder Alex Rivera leads the engineering team; he spent 5 years building real‑time monitoring systems at a cardiac‑device startup.
problem: Sepsis kills 1 in 3 ICU patients, and the median time from onset to antibiotics is still 3‑4 hours, far beyond the 1‑hour window recommended by guidelines.
solution: Our platform streams bedside sensor data, labs and notes into a deep‑learning model that continuously recalibrates risk scores. When a score crosses a calibrated threshold, an alert pops up in the clinician’s workflow, prompting immediate assessment and treatment.

name: DermReach
description: DermReach lets a community health worker in a remote village snap a picture of a skin concern, upload it via a low‑bandwidth app and receive a board‑certified dermatologist’s diagnosis (with AI triage) within 24 hours. We bundle the service with simple follow‑up instructions so patients don’t have to travel hundreds of kilometers for a skin check.
target_customer: District health offices that fund primary‑care posts; they pay because the service prevents costly referrals and reduces the burden of advanced skin disease on limited local resources.
pricing: We charge the health post a flat subscription of $200 / month covering up to 100 consults; extra consults are $8 each. The government‑run tele‑health platform we integrate with pays a per‑consult
traction: Pilot in two PHCs in the Sundarbans (150 consults in 3 months) showed 82 % patient satisfaction and a 30 % reduction in referrals to the district hospital. One local NGO has signed an MoU to roll us out to three additional blocks next quarter.
team: Founder Dr. Aisha Patel, MPH – 8 years as a dermatologist in government hospitals and the lead of a mobile‑clinic program; CTO Ravi Menon, former ML engineer at a national AI lab who built the image‑pre‑processing pipeline.
problem: Rural residents often wait weeks or travel days to see a dermatologist, resulting in delayed treatment, higher morbidity, and unnecessary referrals for simple skin conditions.
solution: A lightweight Android app that works on 2G/3G, lets health workers capture and securely transmit images and patient history; an AI model flags urgent cases, then a network of board‑certified dermatologists reviews the case and returns a diagnosis and care plan through the app.

name: NurseNest
description: NurseNest is a two‑sided marketplace that connects senior‑living operators with vetted, per‑diem nurses. Facilities post shifts, nurses accept them in real time, and the platform handles compliance, credentialing and payment—all in a single dashboard.
target_customer: The primary buyers are senior‑living operators (assisted living, memory‑care, and skilled‑nursing facilities) that need to staff shifts reliably and control labor costs. They pay because traditional agency contracts are opaque, expensive and often leave gaps in coverage.
pricing: We charge a $199/month subscription for each facility (covers up to 50 shifts per month) plus a 12% transaction fee on each nurse‑filled shift. For larger operators we offer custom enterprise plans. Nurses join for free and pay a $15 background‑check fee per credential bundle.
traction: In the last 4 months we’ve piloted with 8 senior‑living sites (total of 250 beds) and placed 320 shifts, cutting average fill time from 48 h to 16 h – a 65 % improvement. The pilots generated $28k in gross merchandise volume and we now have 120 actively vetted nurses on the platform. Two of the sites have signed 12‑month LOIs, and we’re on track for $120k ARR by year‑end.
team: I’m Maya Patel, a former RN supervisor who spent 7 years building staffing teams for a national senior‑living chain and later led product at a SaaS health‑operations startup. Co‑founder Luis Gómez comes from a data‑analytics background (ex‑Palantir, ex‑Kaiser) and built the marketplace engine that powers our real‑time matching.
problem: Senior‑living facilities repeatedly struggle with nurse shortages, high agency fees (often >30% markup), and lack of visibility into who is actually showing up for a shift. The result is chronic understaffing, compliance risk, and inflated labor costs.
solution: NurseNest aggregates a verified pool of per‑diem nurses, lets facilities post shifts instantly, and uses an algorithmic match that accounts for certification, location, and preferred schedules. The platform automates credential verification, shift confirmations, and payouts, giving facilities a transparent, on‑demand staffing channel that’s typically 20‑30% cheaper than traditional agencies.

name: ClinicShift
description: ClinicShift is a platform that helps small doctors' offices move their electronic health records from one system to another. We aim to make the migration process smoother and less risky.
target_customer: The owners or administrators of solo practices and small clinics who need to switch EHR vendors but don’t have in‑house IT staff.
pricing: We plan to charge a one‑time migration fee plus a modest monthly support subscription – roughly $2,000‑$5,000 for the migration and $200‑$500 per month thereafter.
traction: We have spoken with a handful of clinics and have a few informal letters of interest, but no signed contracts or revenue yet.
team: Founded by a former medical office manager who has also done some freelance software integration work.
problem: Small clinics often get stuck with outdated or expensive EHR systems and find it difficult to switch because migration is technically complex and time‑consuming.
solution: Our service provides a guided, semi‑automated migration workflow and offers hands‑on assistance to transfer data, set up the new system, and train staff, promising to reduce downtime and data loss.

name: ValorMind
description: ValorMind is a mobile‑first platform that delivers evidence‑based CBT, guided peer groups, and AI‑driven habit coaching specifically for U.S. veterans dealing with PTSD, depression, and transition stress. We integrate with VA electronic health records so clinicians can prescribe digital content and track progress in one place.
target_customer: Our primary payer is the Department of Veterans Affairs (through its tele‑health budgets) plus private veteran health insurers; secondary users are veterans themselves who may purchase a premium tier for extra coaching.
pricing: We charge the VA a per‑member‑per‑month license ($12 / member) and offer a direct‑to‑consumer subscription at $9 / month (or $90 / year) with a limited free tier.
traction: In the past 3 months we completed a pilot with one VA outpatient clinic (300 active users, 80% weekly engagement) and have a waiting list of 1,200 veterans for our beta. Retention at 30 days is 55%, indicating early product‑market fit but also highlighting the need for more polish.
team: I’m a former Army infantry officer turned clinical psychologist (M.S. Clinical Psychology) and co‑founder; the other two team members are a full‑stack engineer and a UX designer, both recent grads.
problem: Veterans face disproportionately high rates of PTSD and depression, yet existing mental‑health apps are generic, hard to integrate with VA systems, and many veterans feel they don’t speak their language.
solution: ValorMind offers a veteran‑centric curriculum, peer‑moderated support circles, and an AI therapist that personalizes exercises based on VA‑recorded diagnoses and user feedback, delivering care that is both clinically rigorous and culturally resonant.

name: Minderly
description: Minderly is a web‑and‑mobile platform that lets families caring for a loved one with dementia coordinate tasks, share medical information, and access vetted professional support in one place. The product blends a simple care‑plan dashboard with AI‑driven reminders and a moderated peer‑support community.
target_customer: The paying customers are the adult children and spouses who act as primary caregivers; they are willing to pay for anything that reduces the daily coordination burden and helps them avoid costly emergency visits.
pricing: We are currently testing a subscription model that ranges from roughly $15‑$30 per month per household, with a discounted annual option and a pay‑as‑you‑go add‑on for live tele‑consults. The exact tiering and whether we’ll also charge per‑caregiver or per‑patient is still being validated.
traction: In the last 6 months we ran a pilot with 200 families (about 350 active caregivers) that logged an average of 4.2 interactions per day and reported a 30% reduction in missed medication doses. Two regional memory clinics have signed an LOI to refer their patients, and we have a waitlist of 1,200 caregivers for the upcoming beta release.
team: Founder Maya Patel is a former RN with a master's in health informatics and three years leading product at a senior‑care SaaS startup; she is joined by a CTO who spent five years building secure tele‑health platforms for a major hospital system.
problem: Families dealing with dementia spend countless hours juggling appointments, medication schedules, and emotional support, often using fragmented tools like spreadsheets, texting groups, and phone calls, which leads to missed tasks and caregiver burnout.
solution: Minderly centralizes the care ecosystem: a shared dashboard for appointments and meds, AI‑powered reminders that adapt to the patient’s routine, a secure chat for family members, and on‑demand access to certified dementia coaches, all designed to lower friction and keep everyone on the same page.

name: EchoGuard
description: EchoGuard is a SaaS platform that monitors, assesses, and intervenes on hearing health for aging workers in high‑noise industries. We combine a lightweight in‑ear sensor with a cloud‑based analytics dashboard and on‑site audiology coaching to keep hearing loss from becoming a safety and productivity issue.
target_customer: Mid‑size manufacturers and union‑represented trade contractors (50‑300 employees) who pay for the service out of occupational health budgets because they need to meet OSHA hearing‑conservation requirements and reduce workers‑comp claims.
pricing: Subscription per site: $250/month for up to 25 users, plus $15 per additional worker; optional on‑demand audiology consults $120 each. Annual contracts receive a 10% discount.
traction: Pilot with two regional construction firms (total 120 workers) showed a 30% reduction in reported hearing‑related incidents after six months and a 95% sensor compliance rate. Both pilots have signed LOIs for a 12‑month rollout.
team: I’m Maya Patel, former occupational health manager at a union‑affiliated electrical contractor and PhD in biomedical signal processing; co‑founder Luis Ramos built the sensor hardware while working at a medical device startup.
problem: Aging workers in noisy trades often ignore early signs of hearing loss until it compromises safety, yet many employers lack affordable, continuous monitoring that also satisfies regulatory mandates.
solution: We ship a discreet, rechargeable in‑ear sensor that measures sound exposure and real‑time hearing thresholds, feeds data to a dashboard that flags risk, and schedules on‑site hearing checks and personalized protection recommendations, all managed through a single subscription.

name: EquiScreen
description: EquiScreen is a cloud‑based platform that embeds social determinants of health (SDOH) screening directly into Medicaid provider workflows, delivering real‑time risk scores and automated referrals to community resources.
target_customer: State Medicaid agencies and Managed Care Organizations (MCOs) – they fund the platform because it boosts completion of CMS‑required SDOH screens, lowers avoidable hospitalizations, and improves quality scores.
pricing: Subscription per screened member: $0.75‑$1.20 per enrollee per month, with a flat‑fee tier for statewide deployments; implementation and integration services are billed as a one‑time project.
traction: Pilot with two state Medicaid programs covering 30,000 members; completed screenings rose 15% in the first 3 months and ED utilization dropped 12% among high‑risk members. Secured $1.2 M seed round and a letter of intent from a third state for 100k members.
team: Jane Doe (CEO) – former CMS health economist and product lead at Oscar Health; John Smith (CTO) – 12 years building EHR integrations at Epic and co‑founded a health‑data startup.
problem: Medicaid providers struggle to capture SDOH data because existing EHR tools are clunky, referral networks are fragmented, and there is no incentive‑aligned, real‑time workflow to act on the information.
solution: EquiScreen plugs into any major EHR or claims system, prompts clinicians with a 10‑question SDOH questionnaire, calculates a risk score, and auto‑generates referrals to vetted community partners via API, turning data into actionable care pathways.

name: IntakeWell
description: IntakeWell is a SaaS platform that tries to make the first appointment paperwork for behavioral health providers less painful. We combine a simple web form with some basic AI‑assisted triage to get patients into therapy faster.
target_customer: We sell to outpatient mental‑health clinics and private practices that want to reduce admin time, because they are the ones paying for the software and seeing the cost savings on staff hours.
pricing: Subscription‑based, roughly $150‑$300 per therapist per month depending on the number of active users and optional analytics add‑on.
traction: We have a clickable prototype and have spoken with a handful of clinics that said the idea sounded interesting, but we have no signed pilots or revenue yet.
team: Founder is a former therapist turned product manager who has built a couple of small health‑tech tools and is now focusing full‑time on IntakeWell.
problem: Behavioral health providers spend a lot of time manually collecting intake forms, checking insurance eligibility, and routing information, which delays care and adds to clinician burnout.
solution: IntakeWell offers an online intake form that auto‑populates patient records, flags missing information, and gives a very basic risk‑level suggestion to help staff prioritize, aiming to cut the manual admin work by half.

name: TheraDocs
description: TheraDocs is an AI‑powered documentation assistant built for physical therapists. It captures voice notes, auto‑populates SOAP templates, and syncs with the major PT EMR platforms, cutting charting time in half.
target_customer: Physical therapy clinics and independent PTs – they pay because the tool frees up clinician time, reduces billing errors, and helps meet compliance without hiring extra admin staff.
pricing: Subscription per therapist: $49 /mo or $499 /yr. Enterprise tier (10+ therapists, custom integrations) $799 /mo. A free 30‑day trial is offered.
traction: Beta launched with 5 clinics (120 active PTs). Users report a 30 % reduction in documentation time and a 15 % drop in claim rejections. Still early; churn and larger‑scale adoption are unproven.
team: Founder Jane Doe – former PT (5 years clinical) with an M.S. in Computer Science; Co‑founder Alex Smith – ex‑software engineer from a health‑tech startup. The core team is currently two people.
problem: Physical therapists spend up to 40 % of their workday on paperwork, leading to less patient contact, burnout, and frequent billing errors.
solution: TheraDocs lets PTs dictate sessions in natural language; the AI maps speech to a PT‑specific SOAP note, validates coding, and pushes the completed note directly into the clinic’s EMR, eliminating manual entry.

name: MomsMind
description: MomsMind is a mobile‑first platform that delivers evidence‑based postpartum depression (PPD) screening and early‑intervention resources directly to new parents and their care teams. The app integrates with existing EMR systems, nudges users to complete validated questionnaires, and flags high‑risk scores for clinician follow‑up.
target_customer: We sell to health systems, OB‑GYN clinics, and payer networks that want to improve maternal outcomes and reduce costly readmissions. The end‑users are mothers, but the paying entity is the provider or insurer because the app helps them meet quality‑of‑care metrics and avoid downstream costs.
pricing: We’re still iterating on the exact structure. The current thinking is a hybrid model – a per‑patient licensing fee (roughly $8‑$12 per screened mother) plus an optional subscription for analytics dashboards ($199‑$299 /month per practice). We haven’t locked down the final price points yet and plan to adjust based on feedback from pilot partners.
traction: In the last 4 months we’ve completed pilot studies with two hospital systems (total 520 screened mothers) and achieved a 92 % questionnaire completion rate. Early clinician feedback says the risk alerts are actionable, and we’ve secured a letter of intent from a regional health insurer to roll out the solution to 15 clinics next quarter.
team: I’m Dr. Anika Patel, a psychiatrist who spent 8 years treating perinatal mood disorders and led a digital‑health product team at a major EHR vendor. Co‑founder Maya Liu comes from a data‑science background at a health‑analytics startup and built the first prototype in 6 weeks.
problem: Postpartum depression affects roughly 1 in 7 new mothers, yet more than half go undetected because screening is inconsistent, time‑consuming, and not integrated into routine postpartum visits.
solution: MomsMind automates the screening process via a short, clinically validated questionnaire delivered on a smartphone. Scores sync in real time with the provider’s EMR, triggering alerts, care pathways, and tele‑counseling referrals—all while preserving patient privacy and encouraging self‑care.

name: OpioTrack
description: OpioTrack is a SaaS platform that lets small outpatient opioid treatment programs log, visualize, and share patients' recovery milestones in real time. We focus on the day‑to‑day adherence data that clinicians need but can’t reliably capture with paper charts.
target_customer: We sell to boutique opioid treatment clinics (10–30 patients) and state‑run “Recovery Coach” programs that are reimbursed per patient by Medicaid. They pay because the platform reduces staff overtime and improves compliance reporting for funding audits.
pricing: Clinic subscription $300/month for up to 15 active patients, then $20 per additional patient. Optional $1,000/yr analytics add‑on for outcomes reporting to payors.
traction: In the last 4 months we’ve run pilots at two community clinics (total 28 patients) and have logged >1,200 daily check‑ins with a 93% data capture rate. Both sites report a 15% reduction in missed appointments and have asked to convert to paid plans.
team: I’m a former Substance Abuse Clinician turned data engineer (M.S. in Health Informatics, 7 years in OTPs) and my co‑founder is a senior full‑stack developer who previously built EMR integrations for a regional health system.
problem: Small opioid treatment programs rely on manual logs to track daily medication adherence, cravings, and relapse triggers, leading to gaps in data, missed warning signs, and costly audit failures.
solution: OpioTrack replaces paper logs with a mobile‑first dashboard that patients update via a secure app, auto‑syncs to the clinic’s portal, and flags compliance breaches in real time for clinicians to intervene before relapse.

name: OncoClear
description: OncoClear is a SaaS platform that automatically validates oncology billing codes against payer contracts and clinical documentation, flagging errors before claims are submitted. We empower oncology practices to capture full revenue while staying audit‑ready.
target_customer: Hospital oncology departments and private oncology groups – they pay because missed or denied claims directly cut their margins and expose them to compliance risk.
pricing: Subscription per provider seat: $199/month for ≤5 clinicians; $349/month for 6‑20 clinicians; custom enterprise rates for larger networks. No hidden fees, usage‑based discount for high claim volume.
traction: Beta with 3 major cancer centers (totaling 120 providers) shows a 22% lift in clean‑claim rate and 15% reduction in denial cost within 8 weeks. Two practices have already signed annual contracts, generating $48K ARR.
team: Founder Dr. Maya Patel, MD, MPH, spent 6 years as a medical director in oncology finance, building internal audit tools; CTO Alex Rivera previously led data pipelines at a large EHR vendor.
problem: Oncology billing is notoriously complex: dozens of bundled codes, ever‑changing payer rules, and strict documentation requirements lead to 30‑40% claim denial rates, costing practices millions and inviting audits.
solution: Our AI‑driven engine parses treatment plans and pathology reports in real time, cross‑references them with the latest payer policies, and inserts the correct codes or alerts staff before submission, eliminating manual rework and reducing denial risk.

name: RareFlow
description: RareFlow is a platform that tries to streamline the ordering and delivery of specialty drugs for people with ultra‑rare conditions. We aim to connect patients, prescribers and specialty pharmacies through a single interface so that the whole process feels a bit less fragmented.
target_customer: The primary payer is the health plan or insurer that covers the high‑cost specialty medication, because they want to keep administrative overhead low and avoid delays in therapy.
pricing: We charge a flat coordination fee of about $150‑$250 per prescription, plus a small percentage (≈3‑5%) of the drug spend for larger accounts.
traction: We have been talking with a handful of small specialty pharmacies and have a pilot with one regional health system, but we have not yet signed any contracts or generated revenue.
team: I’m a former pharmacy technician turned entrepreneur (M.S. in Health Informatics) and my co‑founder is a software developer with a background in ecommerce platforms.
problem: Patients with rare diseases often face long wait times and confusing paperwork when trying to obtain specialty medications, and providers spend a lot of time coordinating between insurers and pharmacies.
solution: Our web‑based dashboard aggregates prescription orders, insurance authorizations and shipping information, letting providers submit a single request that is automatically routed to the appropriate pharmacy and tracked in real time.

name: CardioHome
description: We run a remote cardiac rehabilitation platform that combines sensor‑enabled home exercise monitoring with live physiotherapist coaching, delivering the 12‑week program insurers require without patients needing to travel. Our mobile app syncs with FDA‑cleared wearables to track heart rate, exertion and adherence, and clinicians intervene only when metrics fall outside safe thresholds.
target_customer: Health insurers / Medicare Advantage plans (who need to meet cardiac rehab referral mandates and can reduce readmission costs) and hospitals that want to outsource post‑discharge care.
pricing: We charge the insurer per completed patient episode – $1,200 for the full 12‑week program, with a $200 per‑patient discount for bundled contracts; an optional $150 per‑patient add‑on provides premium telemetry.
traction: Piloted with one regional health system, enrolling 38 patients; 78% completed the program and 30‑day readmissions dropped 15% versus historical controls, generating roughly $45k ARR. Larger contracts are still in negotiation.
team: I’m a former cardiology fellow turned product manager at a remote‑monitoring startup, and my co‑founder is a certified cardiac rehab physiotherapist with ten years of clinical experience. We supplement ourselves with a part‑time data engineer and a freelance UI designer.
problem: Post‑MI patients are routinely referred to in‑person cardiac rehab, but only about 30% actually attend because of travel, work and transportation barriers, leading to higher readmission rates and poorer outcomes.
solution: CardioHome brings the rehab to the patient’s home: wearable sensors automatically log exercise intensity, and a telehealth dashboard lets clinicians monitor safety and adjust prescriptions in real time, dramatically lowering the friction that prevents enrollment.

name: LegacyLink
description: LegacyLink is a web‑and‑mobile platform that lets patients create, update, and share legally‑binding advance care plans with their families and care teams. We combine a simple, guided wizard with secure document storage and real‑time alerts when a plan is out‑of‑date.
target_customer: We sell to health systems and insurance providers who want to reduce costly emergency‑room admissions and improve patient‑centred care; the end‑users (patients and families) get the service at no direct cost.
pricing: We’re still finalising the model – currently we run a pilot with a per‑member‑per‑month (PMPM) fee ranging from $0.75‑$1.50, with volume discounts for large health plans. We’re also exploring a flat‑fee license for hospitals that prefer a predictable budget, but the exact numbers are still being tested.
traction: In the past 6 months we’ve onboarded 3 regional health systems covering 45,000 members, logged 12,000 completed care plans and seen a 20% reduction in surprise ICU transfers among participants. Our NPS is 68, and two insurers have expressed interest in scaling to their national networks.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, a former ICU physician turned health‑tech entrepreneur (ex‑Google Health), co‑founding with Samir Rao, a SaaS product lead who built scaling platforms at Stripe and a clinical informatics specialist, Dr. Liza Cheng.
problem: Patients often lack an easy, legally‑secure way to document their treatment wishes, leading to fragmented information, delayed decisions, and expensive emergency care when families can’t locate or interpret existing directives.
solution: LegacyLink guides users step‑by‑step through a legally vetted advance directive, stores it in an encrypted cloud, and pushes updates to EMR integrations and designated contacts, ensuring the right information is available exactly when it’s needed.

name: BreatheSense
description: BreatheSense builds a Bluetooth‑enabled inhaler that streams dose‑timing and technique data to a cloud dashboard used by pediatric asthma specialists. The hardware and analytics are bundled into a single, clinic‑managed service.
target_customer: Large pediatric pulmonology practices and hospital asthma programs pay for the service because it gives them objective adherence data to justify therapy changes and reduce ER visits.
pricing: We charge the practice $199 per device per year (includes hardware, connectivity, and analytics) plus a $0.50 per recorded inhalation for high‑volume centres.
traction: In the past 6 months we have deployed 75 devices across three hospital networks, resulting in a 15% reduction in acute exacerbations for the enrolled patients and a letter of intent from a fourth health system for a pilot of 200 devices.
team: Founder Dr. Maya Patel is a former pediatric pulmonology fellow who spent two years developing digital adherence tools at a university lab; CTO Alex Kim previously led firmware for a wearable glucose monitor.
problem: Pediatric asthma specialists lack reliable, real‑time data on whether kids actually use their rescue inhalers correctly, leading to guesswork and preventable ER visits.
solution: Our inhaler records each actuation, pressure curve, and GPS‑stamp, pushes the data to a HIPAA‑compliant dashboard, and alerts clinicians when usage patterns indicate poor control, enabling timely intervention.

name: RadFlow AI
description: RadFlow AI is an AI‑driven radiology workflow platform that automatically triages imaging studies, surfaces high‑risk findings, and routes cases to the right subspecialist. By embedding directly into existing PACS, it cuts report turnaround time by up to 40% while improving diagnostic safety.
target_customer: Hospital radiology departments and large outpatient imaging centers – they pay because the tool reduces backlog, accelerates revenue cycles, and lowers the risk of missed critical findings.
pricing: Subscription per scanner: $2,500 – $4,500 per month tiered by study volume, plus an enterprise‑wide license starting at $25k / year for multi‑site health systems.
traction: Pilot in three academic hospitals (Mayo, UCSF, Johns Hopkins) shows 15% faster TAT and a 10% increase in critical‑findings detection; we have converted two pilots into paying customers and are at $210k ARR with 12 radiologists using the tool daily.
team: Founder Dr. Maya Patel – former chief radiologist at a 750‑bed hospital and Stanford AI fellowship; Co‑founder Alex Kim – ex‑Google Health AI engineer who led large‑scale image‑analysis pipelines.
problem: Radiology departments are overwhelmed by growing study volumes, manual triage creates delays, and critical abnormalities can be missed in the shuffle, leading to poorer patient outcomes and revenue loss.
solution: Our platform plugs into the existing PACS, runs a validated deep‑learning model on every incoming study, flags high‑acuity cases, and auto‑assigns them to the most appropriate subspecialist, giving radiologists a prioritized worklist and decision‑support alerts in real time.

name: MindBridge
description: MindBridge is a mobile app that aims to help employees figure out where to go for mental‑health support. We try to make it easy to discover resources and get a quick sense of what’s available through a company’s benefits program.
target_customer: HR or people‑operations teams at midsize businesses who want to offer something extra for employee wellbeing, usually paid out of the HR budget because it looks good on a culture slide.
pricing: We’re planning a per‑user subscription model, roughly $5‑$10 per active employee per month, with a small discount for larger headcounts.
traction: We’ve run a very informal pilot with one company (about 30 users) and have a few sign‑ups on a waiting list, but no concrete revenue or retention data yet.
team: Founder is a former HR manager who spent a few years in corporate benefits administration and recently took a coding bootcamp to build the product.
problem: Employees often feel lost when trying to navigate mental‑health benefits and resources, leading to low utilization and unmet wellbeing needs.
solution: Our app aggregates the various mental‑health services a company offers into a single, searchable interface and provides simple guidance on how to access them, hoping to reduce friction and improve uptake.

name: CareBridge Health
description: CareBridge Health is a mobile‑first platform that helps uninsured patients with chronic conditions—like diabetes and hypertension—to stay on track with medication, diet, and appointments, using low‑cost SMS reminders and community health worker support.
target_customer: State Medicaid waivers, community health centers, and NGOs that fund care for uninsured patients; they pay per active user because the program reduces emergency visits.
pricing: We charge $0.99 per patient per month for the SMS platform, plus a $200 per month subscription for health centers that want analytics and care‑manager dashboards.
traction: Pilot with two community clinics in Ohio: 180 patients enrolled, 12% reduction in ER visits over 3 months, but user churn is high and other apps are competing for the same space.
team: Founder Dr. Maya Patel, former internist at a public hospital who built a telehealth service for low‑income patients; co‑founder Alex Gomez, a software engineer with two years at a health‑AI startup.
problem: Uninsured patients with chronic diseases lack affordable, coordinated support, leading to medication non‑adherence and costly emergency care.
solution: A lightweight SMS‑based reminder system combined with a web portal for community health workers to monitor vitals, provide education, and triage alerts, all that works on basic phones without data plans.

name: GapBridge Health
description: We built a SaaS platform that identifies, prioritizes, and automates outreach for care gaps in Medicare Advantage (MA) members – from missed preventive screenings to medication adherence. The system plugs directly into carriers’ existing claims and EHR feeds, surfacing actionable alerts for care managers in real time.
target_customer: Our primary customers are Medicare Advantage plans and their affiliated health systems, who pay because closing care gaps reduces costly hospitalizations and improves Star ratings, ultimately protecting their profit margins and bonuses.
pricing: We charge a subscription fee per 1,000 covered lives, plus a usage‑based component for each automated outreach completed. Roughly, plans have seen proposals in the $8‑$12K per 1K lives range, but the exact tiered structure is still being refined as we learn usage patterns.
traction: In pilot pilots with two regional MA carriers we reduced preventable readmissions by 14% and boosted preventive screening compliance by 22% over six months. Both partners have signed letters of intent for broader roll‑outs, but we have yet to finalize a scalable pricing contract.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, former CMS policy analyst turned health data entrepreneur, and co‑founder James Liu, a former senior engineer at a large health‑IT vendor who built large‑scale claims pipelines.
problem: MA plans struggle to identify and close care gaps quickly; data is siloed, workflows are manual, and missed interventions cost millions in avoidable hospital stays and lower Star ratings.
solution: Our platform ingests claims, pharmacy, and clinical data nightly, runs a proprietary risk‑scoring engine to surface the highest‑impact gaps, and triggers automated, HIPAA‑compliant outreach (text, voice, nurse call) that care managers can triage and close—all from a single dashboard.

name: OrthoFlow
description: OrthoFlow is a SaaS platform that streamlines the day‑to‑day clinical operations of independent orthopedic surgery centers – from instrument tracking and case scheduling to postoperative reporting. By digitising the workflows that are still done on paper or in siloed spreadsheets, we cut admin time and reduce costly delays.
target_customer: The paying customer is the surgery center's managing partner or practice administrator, who needs to keep the center profitable while maintaining compliance and surgeon satisfaction.
pricing: We charge a flat monthly subscription of $3,000 per operating room (up to 5 ORs) plus a $250 per‑procedure add‑on for instrument‑tracking analytics. Contracts are annual with a 30‑day free trial.
traction: In the last 4 months we have onboarded 3 independent orthopedic ASCs (total 12 ORs) and have reduced their average pre‑op admin time by 35%. Two of those centers have already renewed for a second year, and we are in talks with a regional ASC network for a pilot.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, former orthopedic resident turned operations manager at a busy ASC where I built the original spreadsheet workflow we now automate. Co‑founder Jake Liu spent 6 years as a product manager at a health‑IT startup and led the UI/UX for their EMR integration.
problem: Independent orthopedic surgery centers still manage instrument sets, case schedules, and post‑op documentation with manual logs, leading to misplaced kits, last‑minute schedule changes, and billing errors that cost $150‑$300 K per year per center.
solution: OrthoFlow replaces those paper logs with a cloud‑based dashboard that syncs instrument inventory with the surgical schedule in real time, auto‑generates turnover checklists, and pushes post‑op data to the center’s billing system, giving staff a single source of truth and cutting admin overhead dramatically.

name: RuralTrial
description: RuralTrial is a mobile platform that brings clinical trial participation to patients in remote and underserved areas. We equip community health workers with a tablet‑based app, a kit of biosensors, and a secure data pipeline so sponsors can run decentralized studies without building a physical site.
target_customer: Pharma and biotech sponsors pay for the service because it unlocks a 30‑40% patient pool that is otherwise unreachable, while local clinics get a revenue share for hosting the trial kit.
pricing: We charge sponsors a per‑patient fee of $250 – $400 (depending on assay complexity) plus a flat monthly platform subscription of $3,000 for each participating clinic.
traction: Pilot with three independent rural clinics in Iowa and Arkansas enrolled 152 participants across two Phase II oncology and cardiology studies, achieving 92% data completion and 84% protocol compliance. Two sponsors have signed LOIs for 2025 roll‑outs, and we received an IND‑friendly data‑integrity certification from the FDA’s Center for Drug Evaluation.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel is a former FDA reviewer (Oncology) and led decentralized trials at a top‑10 CRO; CTO Luis Romero previously built a tele‑health platform that scaled to 200k users in Latin America.
problem: Rural patients are excluded from most trials because sites are too far, travel costs are prohibitive, and sponsors lack a vetted, compliant way to collect high‑quality data outside traditional research centers.
solution: RuralTrial equips local health workers with a plug‑and‑play kit (finger‑stick blood sensor, wearable ECG, GPS‑verified consent) and a HIPAA‑compliant app that streams data in real time to the sponsor’s LIMS, eliminating travel, reducing drop‑out, and delivering regulatory‑grade data from the patient’s home.

name: FlowMed
description: FlowMed is a cloud‑based platform that tries to make hospital supply chains a little more efficient by giving managers a single dashboard for ordering, inventory and usage data. We think the system can help cut down on waste and improve visibility across departments.
target_customer: Our primary buyers are hospital operations directors or supply chain managers who control the purchasing budget, because they are looking for any tool that could potentially reduce spend on consumables.
pricing: We plan to charge a monthly subscription per hospital, roughly $2,000‑$5,000 depending on size, with an optional implementation fee for data onboarding.
traction: We have a prototype and have had informal talks with a few midsize hospitals, but no signed LOIs or pilots yet – just some curiosity from a couple of contacts.
team: I’m a former logistics analyst at a large health system and my co‑founder is a software engineer who worked on ERP integrations for a medical device company.
problem: Hospitals often struggle with fragmented supply data, leading to over‑ordering, stock‑outs and unnecessary waste of expensive consumables.
solution: Our platform aggregates purchase orders, inventory levels and usage logs into a unified view and offers basic alerts when thresholds are crossed, aiming to give managers a clearer picture of what they have and what they need.

name: PulseFeedback
description: PulseFeedback is a SaaS platform that lets outpatient clinics collect, analyze, and act on real‑time patient feedback via a short, structured survey sent right after a visit. The dashboard surfaces the top drivers of satisfaction and flags outliers for immediate follow‑up, helping practices improve quality scores without adding admin overhead.
target_customer: The paying customers are ambulatory care groups and independent clinics that need to meet HEDIS and CMS patient‑experience metrics to avoid penalties and keep referrals flowing.
pricing: We charge a flat monthly fee per provider – $40 per clinician for up to 150 surveys, with a $0.20 per extra survey beyond that. Enterprise bundles start at $3,000/mo for 100+ clinicians and include custom reporting.
traction: In the last 4 months we have piloted with two regional multispecialty clinics (total 85 providers) and have a 78% survey completion rate, with a Net Promoter Score lift of 6 points after a month of using our alerts. The pipeline is at $150k ARR in letters of intent, but we’re still competing with a dozen similar tools.
team: I’m a former senior product manager at a large EMR vendor (10 yrs in clinical workflows) and co‑founder Maya Patel is a data scientist who built analytics for a telehealth startup; we’re currently a two‑person team with a part‑time contractor for UI design.
problem: Outpatient practices struggle to get timely, actionable patient feedback; existing survey tools are either paper‑based, produce vague results, or are locked into costly EHR ecosystems, leading to low response rates and missed quality‑improvement opportunities.
solution: PulseFeedback embeds a 2‑question SMS/ email prompt that patients receive within 30 minutes of checkout, automatically ties responses to the visit in the practice’s scheduling system, and surfaces concise, prioritised insights on a live dashboard so staff can close the loop within 24 hours.

name: SomniSense
description: SomniSense builds a compact, FDA‑cleared home sleep apnea kit that records airflow, oximetry, and snoring patterns and delivers a physician‑reviewed report within 48 hours. Our goal is to make reliable diagnosis as easy as ordering a COVID test.
target_customer: The end‑user is the patient (or their caregiver), but the billable party is usually the employer‑health plan or a primary‑care clinic that wants to triage referrals without sending patients to a sleep lab.
